The tool aimed to assess credibility during interviews by analyzing facial cues and physiological responses, raising concerns about accuracy across ethnicities and potential for biased outcomes. Deployed in pilots in Hungary, Greece, and Latvia, it processed data from EU and third-country nationals.
process_and_outcome: Project concluded in 2020 amid criticism; no widespread deployment due to ethical reviews. EU suspended similar high-risk uses pending AI Act rules, with EDPS issuing warnings on biometric categorization.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (prohibited remote biometric ID)

example_name: European Commission AI Act Application to EU Institutions
example_background: The EU AI Act explicitly extends its scope to Union institutions, bodies, offices, and agencies when they act as providers or deployers of AI systems. This provision ensures that entities such as the European Commission, European Parliament, and various agencies like the European Medicines Agency or Frontex are subject to the same risk-based obligations as private entities. For instance, these institutions deploy AI systems for tasks including document processing, predictive analytics in research funding, border management, and internal decision-support tools. The regulation mandates that high-risk AI systems used by these bodies undergo conformity assessments, risk management, data governance, transparency, and human oversight requirements prior to deployment. This inclusion addresses potential gaps where public sector AI use could otherwise fall outside regulatory oversight, promoting consistent standards across the EU public administration. Documentation during the legislative process highlighted examples of EU agencies piloting AI for procurement, HR management, and cybersecurity, necessitating preparation for compliance frameworks like codes of practice and post-market monitoring.
process_and_outcome: The AI Act was adopted in 2024 with provisions entering into force progressively, requiring EU institutions to conduct impact assessments and register high-risk systems in the EU database by specified timelines. No enforcement actions have occurred yet due to the regulation's recency, but institutions have initiated internal audits and training programs to align deployments.

example_background: The European Border and Coast Guard Agency (Frontex) deploys AI systems for border management, including predictive analytics for migration flows, facial recognition in surveillance operations, and drone-based monitoring integrated with machine learning algorithms. These systems process vast amounts of data from sensors, cameras, and databases to support situational awareness and operational planning across EU external borders. As an EU agency acting as a deployer of AI, Frontex must navigate emerging obligations under the AI Act, particularly for high-risk systems involving biometric categorization and real-time remote biometric identification in publicly accessible spaces. Preparatory work includes ethical AI guidelines and data protection impact assessments conducted in collaboration with national authorities, reflecting the agency's role in large-scale EU-funded projects like the European Border Surveillance System (EUROSUR).
process_and_outcome: Frontex published its AI strategy in 2023, committing to conformity assessments and transparency reporting ahead of AI Act enforcement. Ongoing pilots are being evaluated for risk classification, with plans for registration in the EU AI database upon full implementation.
involved_parties: Frontex (European Border and Coast Guard Agency)

example_name: European Medicines Agency AI in Drug Evaluation
example_background: The European Medicines Agency (EMA) utilizes AI systems as a deployer for accelerating drug evaluation processes, including natural language processing for analyzing clinical trial data, machine learning models for pharmacovigilance signal detection, and predictive tools for assessing manufacturing quality control. These systems handle sensitive health data from submissions across member states, supporting centralized authorization procedures. As an EU agency, EMA's AI deployments fall under the AI Act's scope, requiring high-risk system classifications for those impacting fundamental rights or safety, with obligations for data quality, traceability, and cybersecurity. The agency has piloted AI in its Big Data Steering Group initiatives, processing anonymized datasets to identify adverse events faster than traditional methods.
process_and_outcome: EMA issued guidance in 2023 on AI/ML-based software as medical devices, aligning with AI Act preparation. Systems are undergoing validation, with public pilots demonstrating improved efficiency without reported issues.

example_name: European Data Protection Board AI Accountability Framework
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB), composed of national data protection authorities, provides opinions on AI systems processing personal data, acting as an advisory body to EU institutions. It deploys AI tools internally for data analysis in guideline development and case monitoring, ensuring consistency in GDPR enforcement. As part of EU bodies, these deployments require AI Act compliance, focusing on transparency for general-purpose AI and human oversight in decision-making processes related to privacy assessments.
process_and_outcome: EDPB adopted opinions in 2023-2024 recommending AI Act alignment with GDPR, with internal systems audited for prohibited practices and high-risk obligations.

example_background: The European Union Intellectual Property Office (EUIPO) deploys AI systems to assist in trademark and design examinations, using image recognition and similarity matching algorithms to process over 200,000 applications annually. These tools automate preliminary assessments, flagging potential conflicts based on visual and textual data from the EUIPO database. As an EU office acting as an AI deployer, it must implement risk management systems, maintain detailed logs, and ensure accuracy to avoid erroneous decisions affecting intellectual property rights.
process_and_outcome: EUIPO's AI strategy includes conformity self-assessments and pilot evaluations since 2022, with transparency reports published on system performance.

example_name: European Central Bank AI in Financial Supervision
example_background: The European Central Bank (ECB) uses AI systems for supervisory technology (SupTech), including machine learning for analyzing bank risk data, anomaly detection in reporting, and natural language processing for stress test documentation. These tools support the Single Supervisory Mechanism across eurozone banks, processing terabytes of financial data daily. As a Union institution deploying high-risk AI, the ECB addresses obligations for robustness, bias mitigation, and accountability in outputs influencing monetary policy and stability.
process_and_outcome: ECB published its AI principles in 2022, with ongoing pilots integrated into frameworks compatible with AI Act requirements, including ethical reviews.

example_background: The European Parliament participates in AI4EU, a federation platform providing access to AI resources for public sector innovation, deploying models for legislative text analysis and citizen engagement tools. As a Union institution, it ensures AI systems adhere to risk classifications, with emphasis on transparency for generative AI used in policy drafting support.
process_and_outcome: Parliament's AI initiatives include ethical guidelines and pilot evaluations aligned with AI Act preparatory phases.

example_name: European Environment Agency AI for Climate Modeling
example_background: The European Environment Agency (EEA) deploys AI systems for environmental data analysis, including predictive modeling for climate change impacts and satellite imagery processing for land use monitoring. These high-risk systems inform EU policy-making, requiring data governance and accuracy under the AI Act as the agency acts as a deployer.
process_and_outcome: EEA integrated AI into its Copernicus services with quality assurance protocols matching AI Act standards.

example_name: EU AI Office Establishment and Oversight
example_background: The EU AI Office, established under the AI Act within the European Commission, coordinates compliance for EU institutions deploying AI, providing guidance, codes of practice, and enforcement support. It addresses systemic risks from general-purpose AI models used internally, ensuring all Union bodies register and monitor systems.
process_and_outcome: Launched in 2024, the office has issued initial recommendations for institutional compliance, with no violations recorded.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act, Title X (AI Office)

example_name: Palantir Military Contract with Civilian Data Use
example_background: Palantir Technologies developed AI-powered data analytics platforms initially under contracts with U.S. military and intelligence agencies for defense and national security operations, including predictive analytics for targeting and surveillance. These systems were deployed in military contexts such as support for operations in Afghanistan and integration with U.S. Army systems. Subsequently, the same core technology was adapted and placed on the market for civilian law enforcement and public sector uses, such as predictive policing by police departments in Los Angeles and New Orleans, as well as humanitarian data management for organizations like the United Nations. The dual-use nature involved placing the AI system on the market for both excluded military purposes and non-excluded civilian purposes without fully segregating the deployments, raising questions about compliance obligations when shifting to non-military applications. Palantir's Gotham and Foundry platforms processed vast datasets for pattern recognition, with military versions handling classified defense data while civilian versions managed public records and social services data.
process_and_outcome: U.S. Department of Defense awarded multi-year contracts worth hundreds of millions; civilian contracts faced public scrutiny and lawsuits alleging privacy issues, leading to contract terminations in some cities like New Orleans in 2020 after civil rights audits; no formal regulatory fines under AI-specific rules but ongoing congressional oversight on dual-use AI.
involved_parties: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (national security user)

example_name: Project Maven U.S. DoD AI Drone Program
example_background: In 2017, the U.S. Department of Defense initiated Project Maven to integrate AI for analyzing drone footage and imagery from military operations in conflict zones, aimed at improving target identification and intelligence for defense purposes. Google was contracted to provide TensorFlow-based AI models for object detection in video feeds captured by MQ-9 Reaper drones. The project involved placing AI systems into service exclusively for military purposes, processing petabytes of classified footage to support combat operations. Internal employee protests arose over the military application, leading Google to decline contract renewal despite DoD assurances of non-offensive use. The AI was not marketed or used for civilian purposes during the project tenure.
process_and_outcome: Contract awarded in 2017 for $9 million pilot, expanded, but Google exited in 2018 after employee backlash; DoD shifted to other providers like Amazon; no regulatory enforcement as it remained within military scope.
involved_parties: U.S. Department of Defense (initiator and user)

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition for Law Enforcement and Defense
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition database scraping billions of images from public websites, marketed to U.S. law enforcement agencies for public security and investigations, including ICE and FBI uses. The technology was also pursued by military and national security entities for intelligence purposes, such as identifying individuals in conflict zones. When placed on the market for civilian law enforcement, it fell under general data protection scrutiny, but proposals emerged for modified versions in defense contexts. European authorities investigated due to non-compliance with data rules when used outside strict national security remits, highlighting dual-use risks where military-intended systems were accessed by non-military users.
process_and_outcome: Fined €30 million by Dutch DPA in 2024; banned in several EU countries; ongoing U.S. congressional inquiries; company continued operations with U.S. government contracts.
involved_parties: U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement (national security user)

example_name: Anduril Lattice AI for Border and Military Surveillance
example_background: Anduril Industries created the Lattice AI platform for autonomous surveillance, integrating sensors and drones for real-time threat detection. Initially deployed for U.S. Customs and Border Protection in civilian law enforcement contexts at the southern border, the system was later adapted for military use by the U.S. Army and Marine Corps in defense exercises. The dual deployment involved placing the AI on the market for both non-excluded border security purposes and excluded military operations, with the same software stack processing data from civilian border patrols and combat simulations without full separation.
process_and_outcome: Secured $1 billion+ DoD contracts; CBP deployed systems operational in 2020; faced NGO challenges on privacy but no fines; continued expansion.
involved_parties: U.S. Customs and Border Protection (civilian user)

example_name: NSO Group Pegasus Spyware National Security Deployment
example_background: NSO Group developed Pegasus, an AI-enhanced spyware for mobile device surveillance, marketed exclusively to governments for national security and counter-terrorism purposes. Deployed by entities like Saudi Arabia and UAE intelligence for monitoring dissidents and threats, it was used in military-adjacent operations. Instances emerged where governments repurposed it for non-security purposes, such as targeting journalists, bringing it into civilian oversight scopes. The system used AI for behavioral analysis and zero-click exploits, placed into service solely for excluded purposes but scrutinized when evidence showed broader applications.
process_and_outcome: U.S. blacklisted NSO in 2021; multi-million lawsuits by targets; EU investigations under GDPR; company denied misuse.
involved_parties: Government of Saudi Arabia (national security user)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act national security exclusion

example_name: Amazon Rekognition Sold to Police and Military
example_background: Amazon Web Services offered Rekognition, an AI facial recognition service, placed on the market for general cloud users including civilian law enforcement like Orlando Police Department for suspect identification in public security. The U.S. military explored it for defense applications such as soldier monitoring and drone imagery analysis. Dual-use occurred as the same API was accessible for both excluded military testing and non-excluded police deployments, with AWS marketing it broadly without purpose-specific restrictions.
process_and_outcome: Amazon paused police sales in 2020 for one year after protests; DoD pilot discontinued; no fines but policy changes implemented.

example_name: UK MoD AI Centre for Defence Exclusion
example_background: The UK Ministry of Defence established the Defence AI Centre in 2021 to develop and deploy AI systems exclusively for military purposes, including autonomous systems for logistics, simulation, and decision support in defense operations. These AI tools were put into service within classified military networks, not placed on the market for civilian use, adhering to national security demarcations. Examples include AI for wargaming and predictive maintenance on military hardware.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing program with £million investments; aligned with national AI strategy; no compliance issues reported.
involved_parties: UK Ministry of Defence (developer and user)
Defence Science and Technology Laboratory (technical arm)

example_name: iBorderCtrl EU Border AI Repurposed for Military
example_background: The EU-funded iBorderCtrl project developed AI for automated lie detection and risk assessment at civilian borders using thermal imaging and behavioral analysis. Post-project, components were considered for military checkpoint uses in peacekeeping missions by EU forces. Placing the AI initially for non-excluded law enforcement/public security but evaluating military applications without modification raised dual-use compliance questions.
process_and_outcome: Project completed 2019 with pilots; faced ethical reviews; no widespread military adoption; criticized by EDPS.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act law enforcement exceptions

example_name: Sentient Digital Israel Defense AI Systems
example_background: Sentient Digital, an Israeli firm, provides AI vision systems for military drones and surveillance exclusively to IDF and allied defense forces for national security operations, including real-time object tracking in combat zones. The systems are developed and put into service solely within military frameworks, not marketed for civilian uses, maintaining strict exclusion from general commercial AI regulations.
process_and_outcome: Contracts with IDF ongoing; no regulatory actions as military-only; participated in defense expos.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: UN Export Controls on dual-use tech

example_name: DeepMind Royal Free NHS Data Deal
example_background: In 2016, DeepMind, a Google subsidiary, partnered with the UK's Royal Free NHS Trust to develop Streams, an AI system for predicting acute kidney injury. The system accessed medical records of 1.6 million patients without explicit patient consent, initially framed as a research project. The processing involved real-world clinical data beyond pure scientific development, leading to deployment-like testing without full transparency or data minimization.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated in 2017 and ruled the data processing unlawful due to inadequate legal basis and transparency. No fine was issued, but DeepMind was mandated to revise practices, enhance consent mechanisms, and ensure future projects align with data protection principles. The partnership continued with adjustments.

example_name: EU AI Regulatory Sandbox Launch by Italy
example_background: Italy established one of the first national AI regulatory sandboxes in 2022 under its National AI Strategy, allowing controlled testing of AI systems in real-world conditions prior to full market placement. Participants, including startups developing AI for healthcare and public services, conduct product-oriented research and testing exempt from certain obligations during the sandbox phase, provided they adhere to ethical standards and exit plans.
process_and_outcome: The sandbox, overseen by the Italian Agency for Digital Transformation, admitted initial projects in 2023, enabling safe experimentation. Successful pilots transition to compliant market deployment, with reporting ensuring no undue risks. This initiative demonstrates structured support for R&D without undermining regulatory goals.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (Article 57 on regulatory sandboxes)

example_name: Horizon Europe AI Research Funding Exemption Model
example_background: Under the EU's Horizon Europe program (2021-2027), numerous AI research projects receive funding for scientific R&D activities, such as developing trustworthy AI frameworks or multimodal models. These projects explicitly focus on pre-market development, with outputs remaining internal to research consortia until commercialization. Ethical guidelines mandate compliance with Union law throughout, ensuring research does not transition to service without assessments.
process_and_outcome: Projects like TAILOR and AI4Europe have progressed through funding phases, publishing open-source tools and benchmarks while maintaining research-only status. Annual reporting to the European Commission verifies adherence, with successful models informing future regulations without enforcement actions.
involved_parties: Horizon Europe consortia (e.g., universities, research institutes)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI

example_name: Amazon Rekognition Moratorium and Research Pivot
example_background: Amazon developed Rekognition, a facial recognition AI service, initially through internal R&D and made it available via AWS in 2016 for commercial use. Following civil rights group concerns over bias in law enforcement applications, Amazon announced in 2020 a one-year moratorium on police sales, redirecting efforts toward research improvements like bias mitigation studies conducted in controlled settings before resuming limited offerings.
process_and_outcome: No formal regulatory fine occurred, but public pressure and internal review led to the moratorium extension and enhanced research protocols. Amazon published findings from internal studies, aligning closer with ethical R&D standards while preparing for compliant market re-entry.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: US NIST AI Risk Management Framework

example_name: UK AI Standards Hub Research Testing Framework
example_background: The UK AI Standards Hub, launched in 2021, facilitates collaborative R&D on AI standards through testing environments that simulate real-world conditions without full market deployment. Participants develop assurance techniques for AI systems in sectors like healthcare and autonomous vehicles, ensuring activities remain within recognized ethical and professional standards prior to commercialization.
process_and_outcome: The hub has supported over 50 projects, producing toolkits and guidance adopted by industry. No violations reported; outcomes feed into voluntary assurance schemes, demonstrating effective pre-market compliance navigation.
involved_parties: Member organizations (e.g., BSI, Alan Turing Institute)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (testing in real-world conditions)

example_background: In November 2022, Meta released Galactica, a large language model trained on scientific papers for research purposes. Intended as an internal R&D demo, it was made publicly accessible online, leading to rapid generation of inaccurate content. Meta took it offline within days, recognizing that public deployment blurred lines between research and service, prompting a return to controlled scientific development.
process_and_outcome: Meta voluntarily withdrew the model after 3 days, conducted internal reviews, and shifted to private research betas. No regulatory action ensued, but it served as a case study in responsible AI release practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI

example_background: DeepMind, owned by Alphabet, developed AI capable of generating realistic synthetic videos but faced scrutiny for early experiments lacking disclosure mechanisms. In related projects like healthcare AI, the company processed sensitive patient data with NHS partnerships, prompting questions on risk assessments and transparency in algorithmic decisions affecting medical diagnoses.
process_and_outcome: UK ICO fined DeepMind £500,000 in 2018 for sharing 1.6 million patient records without proper consent. DeepMind responded with a privacy charter and independent audits, improving data handling protocols.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI in healthcare)

example_background: The iBorderCtrl project, funded by the EU Horizon 2020 program, developed an AI system using thermal imaging and lie detection to assess traveler credibility at borders. It aimed to automate risk screening but lacked independent validation of accuracy and raised concerns over false positives affecting vulnerable groups, with limited transparency on algorithmic decision-making.
process_and_outcome: Pilot tests concluded in 2019 with mixed results; the project ended without full deployment. EU Parliament called for stricter oversight, influencing later biometric regulations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (prohibited practices in biometric categorization)

example_name: IBM Watson for Oncology Transparency Issues
example_background: IBM Watson for Oncology, launched around 2016, aimed to assist cancer treatment recommendations. Memorial Sloan Kettering clinicians noted in 2018 that Watson provided unsafe suggestions, such as recommending unproven treatments due to training data limitations from academic centers not reflecting community practices. Lack of explainability hindered clinician trust.
process_and_outcome: IBM pivoted focus in 2020, rebranding health AI efforts. No fines, but led to industry emphasis on validation and transparency in healthcare AI.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (AI HLEG) - transparency, technical robustness and safety

example_background: In 2019, SAP formed a global AI ethics board to guide development of AI products like SAP Leonardo, ensuring alignment with principles such as human oversight and fairness. The board includes ethicists, academics, and executives reviewing high-impact projects. SAP integrated AI ethics into product lifecycle, including bias audits and explainability tools.
process_and_outcome: SAP published AI ethics principles and annual transparency reports, adopting practices like diverse training data and human-in-the-loop for critical decisions, positioning as industry leader.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (AI HLEG)
EU AI Act codes of conduct

example_name: EU AI Act Stakeholder Codes of Conduct
example_background: Following the AI HLEG guidelines, organizations like the European Association for AI (EURAI) and industry groups drafted voluntary codes of conduct in 2023-2024, incorporating principles like transparency labeling for AI-generated content and robustness testing. These serve as precursors to AI Act Article 56 requirements.
process_and_outcome: Codes adopted by members, with self-certification mechanisms; monitored by Commission for effectiveness.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (AI HLEG)

example_background: OpenAI established safety teams post-2018 to address risks in models like GPT series, implementing techniques such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for alignment, red-teaming for robustness, and transparency reports on model cards detailing limitations and biases.
process_and_outcome: Deployed ChatGPT with safeguards in 2022-2023, publishing system cards and engaging external audits, reducing harmful outputs over iterations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Ethics Guidelines for Trustworthy AI (AI HLEG) - accountability, technical robustness

example_background: Amazon's Rekognition service, a cloud-based facial recognition tool, was tested by U.S. law enforcement agencies including the Washington County Sheriff's Office in Oregon. Tests revealed error rates up to 2% for lighter-skinned males but 34% for darker-skinned females, with documented instances of misidentifying members of Congress, particularly women of color, as criminal suspects. Reports highlighted risks of inferring race or other protected characteristics from biometric categorizations, amplifying biases in policing.
process_and_outcome: ACLU organized a campaign leading to a temporary moratorium on facial recognition use by federal agencies in 2021. Amazon restricted police sales for one year in 2020 and updated bias detection features. No direct fines were imposed, but public pressure led to policy changes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act high-risk AI systems

example_background: IBM's Watson AI platform for recruitment analyzed facial expressions and biometrics from video interviews to assess candidate suitability. Studies and employee reports indicated the system penalized women and minorities due to biased training data, effectively inferring gender and race which influenced hiring decisions. The tool categorized candidates based on inferred traits rather than explicit labels.
process_and_outcome: IBM discontinued facial analysis features in 2020 amid backlash. Internal audits revealed compliance gaps, leading to a pivot away from emotion AI. No formal regulatory fines, but it contributed to broader industry scrutiny.

example_background: Microsoft developed facial recognition software capable of detecting emotions, age, gender, and other traits from biometric data. Internal debates emerged over potential misuse to infer psychological states or sensitive beliefs, prompting researchers to publish findings on risks of inferring protected characteristics like political leanings from facial cues.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft chose not to commercialize emotion API in 2022, citing ethical concerns. The company implemented voluntary safeguards and published research on limitations, aligning with emerging standards ahead of regulations.

example_background: Faception provided AI software to Israeli security forces at airports, categorizing individuals into behavioral types such as 'terrorist' or 'extrovert' based on facial biometrics and gait analysis. The system aimed to infer potential threats, which could indirectly reference ethnicity or ideological leanings through probabilistic modeling from facial features.
process_and_outcome: The project faced international criticism but continued under national security exemptions. No EU-level enforcement as it was outside jurisdiction, though it drew parallels to prohibited practices in global discussions.

example_name: EU Law Enforcement Image Filtering Practices
example_background: Europol and national police forces in the EU use biometric databases like the Prüm system for sorting and matching images based on objective traits such as eye color, hair color, and facial structure to aid in criminal investigations. These systems label datasets lawfully acquired under national laws without inferring sensitive personal beliefs or orientations.
process_and_outcome: Operations comply with existing frameworks, with ongoing audits ensuring data minimization. No enforcement actions recorded; systems expanded under Interpol and EU initiatives.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act exceptions for law enforcement

example_background: HireVue's video interview platform employed AI to analyze facial movements, tone, and biometrics to score candidates on traits like conscientiousness. Analysis showed correlations with inferred gender and race, leading to disparate outcomes for protected groups, as the system categorized based on patterns that reflected demographic biases.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, HireVue removed facial and voice analysis amid EEOC scrutiny and public pressure, shifting to gamified assessments. No fine issued, but it marked a retreat from biometric inference in HR.

example_background: SAP's SuccessFactors HR software initially incorporated AI for candidate screening with biometric elements but underwent redesign to avoid inferences on sensitive attributes. The company focused on anonymized data processing and explicit non-inference of protected characteristics like beliefs or orientations.
process_and_outcome: SAP published AI ethics guidelines in 2020, ensuring compliance through third-party audits. The platform achieved certifications and avoided regulatory challenges.

example_background: Worldcoin scanned irises using handheld 'Orb' devices worldwide to create unique biometric identifiers for cryptocurrency distribution. The process collected sensitive biometric data, raising issues about potential downstream uses for inferring demographic or ideological traits via large-scale databases.
process_and_outcome: Bavarian data protection authority in Germany fined Worldcoin €25,000 in 2023 preliminarily; Kenya suspended operations in 2023 over data concerns; multiple countries investigated under GDPR. Worldcoin adjusted privacy notices but continued expansion.

example_background: In 2017-2018, websites hosted AI-generated videos superimposing celebrities' faces, such as Taylor Swift, Scarlett Johansson, and Gal Gadot, onto bodies in adult films. These deepfakes were created using generative adversarial networks (GANs) trained on publicly available images and videos scraped from the internet. The content proliferated on platforms like Reddit and dedicated deepfake sites, reaching millions of views. Creators and distributors operated anonymously, often sharing tools and datasets to enable further production, which amplified the non-consensual use of individuals' likenesses.
process_and_outcome: Reddit banned deepfake pornography subreddits in 2018 following public outcry. Platforms like Pornhub implemented detection tools and content removal policies. US states such as Virginia, Texas, and California enacted laws prohibiting non-consensual deepfake pornography, with some cases leading to criminal charges. Advocacy groups pushed for federal legislation.

example_background: TikTok's app collected biometric data, location information, and behavioral data from users under 16 through features like face filters, music preferences, and video interactions. Internal audits revealed that child accounts were served content and ads based on inferred interests without adequate parental controls or consent mechanisms. The platform's algorithm amplified engaging but potentially harmful content to minors, including challenges that led to injuries or worse.
process_and_outcome: The US FTC settled with TikTok in 2019 for $5.7 million, the largest fine ever for child privacy violations. In 2023, a $1.5 billion settlement was proposed in Illinois BIPA litigation. The EU opened GDPR investigations, and TikTok implemented age-gating and parental controls in response to regulatory pressure.

example_background: China's National Social Credit System integrates data from government agencies, financial institutions, and surveillance cameras to assign scores to citizens based on behavior such as bill payments, social interactions, and compliance with laws. AI algorithms analyze this data to determine access to services like travel, education, and employment. Pilots in cities like Rongcheng used facial recognition and big data to enforce rules, affecting millions by restricting high-speed rail tickets or loans for low scorers.
process_and_outcome: The system expanded nationwide by 2020, with ongoing refinements. International criticism from human rights groups led to UN reports on privacy implications, but domestic enforcement continued without major interruptions. Some pilots were scaled back due to public feedback.

example_name: Meta's AI Content Moderation Best Practices
example_background: Meta developed AI systems to detect and remove harmful content, including hate speech, misinformation, and exploitative material targeting vulnerable groups. Tools like the AI-driven moderation classifiers process billions of posts daily, using natural language processing and computer vision to prioritize human review for nuanced cases. During elections, these systems identified and demoted manipulative deepfakes and coordinated inauthentic behavior campaigns.
process_and_outcome: The systems reduced hate speech prevalence by 75% in some regions per Meta's transparency reports. Independent audits by Stanford Internet Observatory validated effectiveness. Meta published annual reports detailing improvements and shared datasets with researchers to advance industry standards.

example_background: Google partnered with the US Department of Defense on Project Maven to provide AI for analyzing drone footage using object detection models. Employee protests highlighted risks of enabling surveillance and targeting in conflict zones, leading to over 3,000 staff signing a petition against military AI applications that could undermine human dignity.
process_and_outcome: Google terminated the contract in 2018 and established AI principles prohibiting weapons development. The company enhanced ethical review boards for government contracts, setting a precedent for tech firms limiting dual-use AI technologies.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act military exemptions with safeguards

example_background: OpenAI implemented safety measures for models like GPT series, including red-teaming exercises, content filters to prevent generation of harmful instructions, and phased rollouts with usage monitoring. The Superalignment team focused on long-term risks of advanced AI systems manipulating users or infringing on rights through deceptive outputs.
process_and_outcome: Public reports detailed mitigations reducing jailbreak success rates by over 90%. Partnerships with regulators for preparedness frameworks advanced industry-wide standards. No major incidents of prohibited misuse reported from core models.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act general-purpose AI obligations

example_name: Amazon Rekognition Use by Law Enforcement Scrutiny
example_background: Amazon Web Services offered Rekognition, a facial recognition service, to police departments in the United States, including a one-year pilot with the Orlando Police Department in 2017. The system allowed officers to upload photos of suspects or persons of interest and match them against mugshot databases or public images. Concerns arose over accuracy issues, particularly higher error rates for people of color, and the potential for widespread deployment without strict limits on usage scenarios. The pilot involved real-time analysis capabilities tested in public spaces, extending beyond immediate threats to life or specific serious crimes listed in frameworks like the European Arrest Warrant categories. Amazon defended the technology but faced internal employee protests and external advocacy campaigns.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, Amazon announced a one-year moratorium on police use of Rekognition following public pressure. The ACLU and others highlighted risks through testing reports showing misidentifications. No direct EU fines occurred, but it influenced EU policy debates. Several US cities banned or restricted its use.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (prohibited practices for law enforcement)

example_name: Dutch Police SyRI System Court Ban
example_background: The Dutch government implemented the System Risk Indication (SyRI) in 2014, an algorithm-based system integrating data from tax authorities, social services, and other agencies to generate risk profiles for welfare fraud detection. Deployed in certain neighborhoods, it used predictive analytics on personal data, including potential biometric elements in linked databases, to flag suspicious patterns for law enforcement follow-up. The system operated without individual notification and covered broad fraud categories not limited to severe crimes punishable by at least four years imprisonment. Residents challenged it, arguing disproportionate intrusion into privacy for non-serious offenses.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the District Court of The Hague ruled SyRI unlawful, ordering its cessation due to privacy violations. The court found insufficient transparency and proportionality. The government appealed but later discontinued the program in 2021, prompting legislative reforms.

example_name: UK Police Facial Recognition Trials Oversight Report
example_background: Between 2016 and 2020, UK police forces including London Metropolitan Police conducted live facial recognition trials at public events, scanning crowds in real-time to match faces against watchlists. Deployment occurred at events like sports matches and protests, targeting individuals for minor offenses or general intelligence gathering, not strictly limited to imminent threats to life, terrorist attacks, or serious crimes with high custodial sentences. The Biometrics and Surveillance Camera Commissioner reviewed operations, noting varying watchlist criteria and high volumes of scans on bystanders.
process_and_outcome: A 2020 report by the Commissioner found legal basis under existing laws but recommended stricter guidelines. Courts dismissed some challenges, but the IOPC investigated specific deployments. Policy evolved with new codes of practice in 2023 emphasizing necessity for serious crimes.

example_name: ENISA Report on Law Enforcement Biometric Use in Critical Infrastructure Protection
example_background: The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) documented cases where biometric systems were used by authorities to protect critical infrastructure under Directive (EU) 2022/2557, such as airports and ports. In one scenario, real-time facial recognition was deployed at EU border checkpoints during heightened threat periods to identify suspects of terrorism-related offenses punishable by over four years detention. Usage was confined to imminent threats to physical safety, with strict logging and post-event audits.
process_and_outcome: ENISA's 2023 threat landscape report praised the narrowly scoped implementation as aligned with necessity principles, recommending it as a model. No enforcement actions; instead, it informed best practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (exception for critical threats)

example_name: INTERPOL Biometric Hub for Serious Crime Searches
example_background: INTERPOL operates a Biometric Hub launched in 2022, allowing member states to submit facial images for matches against global databases, focused on locating suspects of 32 serious crimes listed in Framework Decision 2002/584/JHA, such as human trafficking and terrorism, all carrying minimum four-year sentences. Usage requires justification of necessity for substantial public interest, with real-time capabilities limited to urgent cases like missing child searches or terrorist threats.
process_and_outcome: The system processed thousands of queries by 2024 with positive hit rates in fugitive identifications. Audits confirmed adherence to data minimization, serving as a compliance benchmark.

example_name: Facial Recognition at Euro 2024 Security Operations
example_background: During the UEFA Euro 2024 tournament in Germany, police used retrospective facial recognition on stadium CCTV footage to identify suspects of violent crimes committed during matches, such as assaults punishable by custodial sentences over four years under German law. Deployment was post-event analysis, not real-time indiscriminate scanning, targeted at known incidents linked to public safety threats.
process_and_outcome: German data protection authorities reviewed and approved the limited use in 2024, resulting in over 150 identifications without broader privacy complaints upheld.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act serious crime exceptions

example_name: Microsoft Azure Face API Law Enforcement Moratorium
example_background: Microsoft restricted sales of its Azure Face API to US police departments in 2020, citing ethical concerns over potential misuse in real-time public surveillance beyond exceptional cases like active shooter responses or child abductions. The decision followed internal reviews and public debates on biometric tools in predictive policing.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft implemented a moratorium and ethical guidelines, avoiding regulatory penalties and positioning as a responsible provider.

example_name: EDPB Guidelines on Biometrics for Victim Searches
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) issued guidelines in 2021 endorsing limited use of remote biometric identification by law enforcement for searching missing children or victims of serious crimes, provided it meets strict necessity and proportionality tests aligned with annex-listed offenses.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines adopted by member states, facilitating compliant implementations without noted violations.

example_background: Epic Games' Fortnite battle royale game employed algorithmic techniques to maximize player session length and spending, particularly targeting younger players. Features like battle passes, loot boxes, and scarcity timers created FOMO (fear of missing out), with algorithms adjusting rewards and notifications based on user behavior data. The system analyzed play patterns to send personalized push notifications and in-game prompts that encouraged continued play or purchases, exploiting addictive loops studied in behavioral psychology. Children were particularly affected due to underdeveloped impulse control, with reports of excessive playtime and microtransactions totaling billions. The design used variable reward schedules akin to slot machines, making outcomes unpredictable and compelling, often without parental oversight.
process_and_outcome: In 2022-2023, the FTC settled with Epic for $520 million: $245 million in refunds for unwanted purchases and $275 million for privacy violations plus restrictions on child data use. EU probes under DSA and multiple lawsuits addressed addictive design.

example_background: TikTok's For You Page algorithm curates short videos based on user interactions, dwell time, and biometric data like video completion rates, creating hyper-personalized feeds that adapt in real-time to keep users scrolling. The system prioritizes content maximizing engagement, often amplifying sensational or emotionally charged videos, leading to excessive screen time especially among minors. Internal documents revealed awareness of addiction risks, with features like autoplay and infinite scroll designed to exploit dopamine responses. In the Netherlands, the algorithm was found to distort behavior by promoting harmful content to children, impairing healthy habits without user control over recommendations.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA fined TikTok €345 million in 2024 for violating children's data rights under GDPR, citing insufficient safeguards against behavioral nudges. Additional probes in EU and US ongoing.

example_name: UK ASA Adjudication on Clixie App
example_background: Clixie's mobile app used gamified elements and notifications to drive user engagement, with algorithms sending timed prompts and rewards to encourage repeated checks and interactions. The Advertising Standards Authority (ASA) reviewed complaints that these features unduly influenced behavior, particularly among younger users, by creating habits through unpredictable rewards and social comparisons. The app's design leveraged behavioral economics principles like loss aversion and instant gratification, making it difficult for users to disengage.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, ASA ruled the app breached advertising rules on undue pressure, requiring changes to marketing claims and features.

example_background: Google developed Federated Learning of Cohorts (FLOC) as part of Privacy Sandbox to replace third-party cookies with privacy-preserving ad targeting. Instead of individual tracking, it groups users into cohorts of 1,000+ based on shared browsing interests computed locally on devices via federated learning. This approach aimed to maintain ad effectiveness while limiting personal data exposure and manipulative micro-targeting, subjecting it to public review and regulatory feedback to ensure no subliminal behavioral distortion.
process_and_outcome: After trials and critiques, Google paused FLoC in 2022, pivoting to Topics API, demonstrating iterative compliance with privacy standards and avoiding prohibited practices.

example_name: FTC Action Against Everalbum Photo App
example_background: Everalbum's app collected facial images and geolocation data without clear consent, using AI to create facial recognition profiles and shareable albums. The system analyzed photos to infer user networks and behaviors, enabling targeted content suggestions that influenced sharing habits subtly through algorithmic recommendations.
process_and_outcome: FTC settled in 2019 with $150,000 civil penalty and orders to delete models, implement privacy programs.

example_name: EU EDPS Probe into EUROPOL Biometric Database
example_background: EUROPOL maintained a facial image database used for identification in law enforcement, including processing from public surveillance sources. The database grew to include millions of images without fully delineated proportionality measures for real-time uses in public spaces. Concerns involved lack of targeted scope, insufficient safeguards for rights impacts, and no mandatory registration or prior impact assessments for all deployments.
process_and_outcome: European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) initiated inquiry in 2022 into compliance with data minimization and proportionality. EUROPOL adjusted data retention policies and enhanced oversight in response.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act biometric identification rules

example_background: Hamburg police used mobile facial recognition units during specific events like Reeperbahn Festival, scanning crowds against narrow watchlists of identified suspects only. Prior to deployment, authorities conducted and published a fundamental rights impact assessment evaluating necessity, proportionality, time/geographic limits, and safeguards. Use was logged in a national database with strict post-use data deletion.
process_and_outcome: Data protection authorities approved following assessment. No incidents of overreach reported; served as model for compliant law enforcement use.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 5 biometric provisions

example_name: CNIL Authorization for Law Enforcement AFR in France
example_background: French CNIL authorized limited use of real-time facial recognition by law enforcement in public spaces for specific enumerated situations like searching for missing persons or terrorism suspects. Requirements included prior FRIA, registration in national database, strict temporal/geographic/personal limits, and proportionality analysis tied to threat evidence.
process_and_outcome: Guidelines issued in 2023 specifying conditions; deployments proceeded under oversight with no violations noted in initial uses.

example_background: The Social Credit System in China, implemented by the government since 2014, assigns scores to individuals and businesses based on a wide range of behaviors including financial reliability, legal compliance, social conduct, and online activities. Data is aggregated from government databases, financial records, cameras, and apps to create profiles that track trustworthiness over time. Low scores result in restrictions such as travel bans, reduced access to loans, school admissions for children, and public shaming on billboards. By 2019, over 26 million 'dishonest' individuals faced flight bans and 2.5 million were barred from high-speed rail. The system uses AI algorithms to process vast datasets from multiple contexts, leading to scores that influence unrelated areas like employment and housing. Private companies like Sesame Credit (Alibaba) participate with voluntary scoring that affects service access. This has raised concerns about profiling based on inferred behaviors across contexts.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing implementation with periodic reports; in 2020, the People's Bank of China ended a pilot credit reference system due to evaluation issues. No major shutdowns, but international criticism persists. Adjustments made to focus on corporate rather than personal scoring in some regions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Planning Outline for the Construction of a Social Credit System (2014-2020)

example_background: In Xinjiang province, Chinese authorities deployed AI-powered facial recognition systems integrated with social scoring from 2017 onward, scanning millions via 20,000+ cameras linked to the Integrated Joint Operations Platform (IJOP). The system collects data on Uighur minorities' movements, phone usage, electricity consumption, and religious practices, assigning scores based on deviations from norms. Low scores trigger police visits, detentions in camps, or restrictions. Over 1 million Uighurs reportedly affected, with AI inferring behaviors from multimodal data across daily life contexts, leading to group-based exclusions unrelated to specific incidents.
process_and_outcome: UN reports in 2022 labeled it potential crimes against humanity; US imposed sanctions on companies like Hikvision in 2019. China denies abuses; no domestic enforcement.

example_name: New York City ADS Predictive Policing
example_background: New York City's Administrative Code Section 14-186, enacted in 2021, addressed the Domain Awareness System (ADS) used by NYPD, which included predictive policing tools scoring locations and individuals based on past arrests, stops, and social data. From 2008, ADS aggregated data from cameras, license plate readers, and crime reports to flag 'hotspots' and persons, often correlating with minority neighborhoods. This led to over-policing, with Black and Latino communities facing higher scrutiny based on historical data patterns across contexts, resulting in disproportionate stops unrelated to current behaviors.
process_and_outcome: 2021 law banned automated scoring of individuals predictive of criminality; NYPD required to disclose AI use. Audit showed prior biases; compliance enforced via public advocate oversight.

example_background: iBorderCtrl, an EU-funded project from 2016-2019, developed AI lie detection using facial micro-expressions, eye tracking, and thermal imaging at borders to score travelers' credibility. It inferred truthfulness from involuntary signals combined with questionnaire data on travel history and intent, classifying individuals for further scrutiny. Piloted at Hungarian, Greek, and Latvian borders, it raised concerns over false positives affecting vulnerable groups like refugees, with scores influencing entry decisions across unrelated personal contexts.
process_and_outcome: Project ended in 2019 without full deployment; criticized by EDPS for fundamental rights risks. No fines, but informed stricter EU AI guidelines.

example_background: Sesame Credit, launched by Ant Financial (Alibaba) in 2015, scores Chinese users from 350-950 based on Alibaba shopping history, payment timeliness, social network connections, and shared data like photos. High scores unlock perks like easier loans, VIP services, and dating app matches; low scores limit rentals or travel bookings. By 2018, 520 million users participated, with scores propagating across platforms, affecting opportunities in unrelated social spheres based on multifaceted behavioral data.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary program continues; integrated into broader social credit. People's Bank revoked license plans in 2018 for data security issues, leading to reforms.

example_background: Chicago's Alternative Policing Strategy (ARRAY), implemented 2012-2019, used AI to score individuals for gang affiliations and violence risk based on police data, social networks, arrests, and locations. Over 400,000 people flagged, mostly in Black and Latino areas, leading to increased surveillance and stops. Scores derived from cross-context data like family ties and past associations influenced policing priorities, resulting in community-wide impacts.
process_and_outcome: Terminated in 2019 after Inspector General report found biases and inefficacy; city settled lawsuits and reformed data use.

example_name: EU AI Act Prohibited Practices Prohibition
example_background: The EU AI Act, finalized in 2024, explicitly prohibits social scoring by public or private actors, defining it as AI evaluating persons based on social behavior data leading to detrimental unrelated treatment. This builds on prior cases like facial recognition bans in public spaces (2021 moratorium). Member states must enforce by 2026, with guidelines preventing systems like COMPAS equivalents.
process_and_outcome: Regulation adopted March 2024; bans effective 2026. Preemptive compliance by firms avoiding prohibited categories.

example_name: California AB 331 AI Employment Bias Audit
example_background: California's 2022 law requires audits for AI tools in employment decisions scoring candidates on inferred traits from resumes, social media, and behavioral data. Companies like HireVue faced scrutiny for video interview analysis inferring personality and risk, potentially excluding groups based on accents or expressions across contexts.
process_and_outcome: Law effective 2023; led to vendor audits and tool modifications. Civil Rights Department enforces with fines up to $100k.

example_name: South Wales Police RIPA Judgment on AFR Locate
example_background: South Wales Police implemented Automatic Facial Recognition (AFR) Locate technology in trials from 2017, using retrospective and real-time capabilities to scan faces in public spaces against watchlists during events like sports matches and festivals. The system processed live feeds from cameras, comparing attendees to databases of persons of interest. A 2018 deployment at the Swansea football match scanned 500 faces in 12 minutes. The technology was integrated into routine policing operations. Challenges highlighted inclusion criteria opacity, potential biases in matching algorithms affecting ethnic minorities and women, continuous scanning evoking surveillance feelings, and risks to freedom of assembly without sufficient safeguards.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, the Court of Appeal ruled the use unlawful, finding no clear legal basis under RIPA and inadequate public consultation. The police revised policies, limiting deployments and improving transparency. The UK government responded with proposed regulations for biometric tools.

example_name: New Orleans Police Department Fusus Surveillance Network
example_background: The New Orleans Police Department (NOPD) partnered with Fusus in 2020 to create a real-time crime center integrating thousands of public and private cameras with AI-powered facial recognition and license plate readers. The system enabled law enforcement to monitor public spaces continuously, querying live feeds for biometric matches. By 2023, it encompassed over 4,000 cameras citywide. Usage included tracking individuals in real-time during investigations. Civil liberties groups noted risks of biased identifications, disproportionate impacts on minority communities, pervasive surveillance in public areas, and limited avenues for contesting real-time decisions.
process_and_outcome: The ACLU and NAACP sued in 2022, alleging constitutional violations. A federal judge issued a preliminary injunction in 2023 halting certain uses pending review. NOPD implemented partial restrictions but continued operations under oversight. Ongoing litigation addresses systemic issues.

example_name: EU Parliament Ban on Real-Time Remote Biometric ID
example_background: During the EU AI Act legislative process from 2021-2024, the European Parliament advocated for and secured a categorical prohibition on real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces for law enforcement, except narrowly defined exceptions like terrorism prevention. This reflected extensive debates on documented risks from existing deployments, including biased error rates (e.g., NIST studies showing higher failures for Black and Asian faces), mass surveillance effects on populations, and interference with rights like assembly. The provision aimed to preempt harms seen in trials across member states.
process_and_outcome: The EU AI Act was adopted in 2024, with Article 5 banning such uses effective 2026, establishing a harmonized high-risk framework. Enforcement delegated to national authorities with fines up to €35 million.

example_name: NIST Face Recognition Vendor Test Bias Findings
example_background: The U.S. National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) conducted the Face Recognition Vendor Test (FRVT) from 2018-2023, evaluating over 100 algorithms from vendors like NEC, IDEMIA, and Amazon on demographic differentials. Tests used datasets of over 18 million images, revealing false positive rates up to 100 times higher for Asian and African American faces compared to Caucasian faces in one-to-one matching, and significant age and sex biases. These findings were applied to law enforcement contexts, including real-time public space identification, underscoring risks of discriminatory effects in operational use.
process_and_outcome: NIST published reports recommending mitigation strategies like demographic benchmarking. Vendors adjusted algorithms; U.S. agencies like FBI incorporated findings into policies, restricting high-risk deployments.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: U.S. Executive Order on AI fairness

example_name: Nice Airport Real-Time Facial Recognition Pilot
example_background: In 2018-2019, French authorities at Nice Côte d'Azur Airport trialed real-time facial recognition on 240 volunteers entering restricted areas, but the technology was designed for scalable public space use. The pilot involved live camera scans matching against passport photos, with reported 90% accuracy. CNIL reviewed it amid broader concerns over expanding to public zones, citing risks of technical errors leading to misidentifications, especially across demographics, immediate enforcement actions, and surveillance normalization.
process_and_outcome: CNIL authorized the limited trial but warned against generalization. No fines issued, but it informed stricter EU guidelines. Deployment not expanded to public enforcement.

example_name: Woodbridge, New Jersey Police FRT Settlement
example_background: Woodbridge Police used facial recognition technology on public social media and surveillance images for investigations, including real-time crowd scanning. A 2022 ACLU investigation revealed scans of 30,000+ faces monthly, with undisclosed vendor algorithms prone to biases per NIST data, affecting enforcement in public spaces.
process_and_outcome: Settled lawsuit in 2023; police agreed to cease use, destroy data, and implement oversight. Paid $210,000 in fees.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: New Jersey state constitution privacy protections

example_name: Facial Recognition at 2022 FIFA World Cup Qatar
example_background: Qatar authorities installed over 3,000 facial recognition-enabled surveillance cameras in stadiums and public areas around World Cup venues to monitor crowds in real-time. The system integrated with a national database for identifying fans, detecting prohibited items, and flagging suspicious behavior. Deployment occurred without public disclosure of prior judicial authorizations per use case, amid broader use of AI for security during the event attended by millions. Data was processed centrally, with real-time alerts to law enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Human Rights Watch and Amnesty International documented the deployment and raised oversight concerns; no formal enforcement actions or fines reported due to jurisdictional issues. Post-event reviews noted extensive surveillance but no legal challenges succeeded.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act remote biometric rules (analogous)

example_background: Following the 2016 Bastille Day truck attack in Nice that killed 86 people, French authorities expanded video surveillance with facial recognition capabilities across public spaces, including promenades and event areas. Systems were deployed for real-time identification using national databases, often justified under urgency provisions post-terror incidents. Initial rollouts bypassed standard prior authorizations, with retrospective reviews by prefectures rather than judicial bodies. By 2023, over 100 cities adopted similar tech.
process_and_outcome: CNIL investigated and in 2019 blocked full deployment pending legal framework; a 2021 law authorized regulated use with judicial warrants. No major fines, but ongoing audits required for compliance.

example_name: EU Law Enforcement AI Pact Signatories
example_background: In April 2024, 29 EU law enforcement agencies signed the AI Pact, a voluntary initiative to align with EU AI Act requirements ahead of full enforcement. Participants committed to risk assessments, transparency, and authorization processes for high-risk AI including real-time biometrics in public spaces. Examples include structured deployment protocols with prior judicial review for non-urgent cases and documented urgency justifications.
process_and_outcome: Ongoing self-certification and audits; no enforcement actions as it precedes mandatory compliance. Serves as a model for adherence to upcoming regulations.
involved_parties: National police authorities (e.g., German Federal Police, Spanish Guardia Civil)

example_name: Hertfordshire Police Facial Recognition Policy Update
example_background: After UK court rulings, Hertfordshire Constabulary implemented a revised policy for live facial recognition use in public spaces, requiring pre-deployment authorization from an independent oversight board modeled on judicial review. Deployments limited to targeted operations with defined watchlists, data deletion post-use, and no adverse decisions based solely on matches. Pilots in 2023 at events included 24-hour retrospective reporting where urgency applied.
process_and_outcome: Policy validated by UK Biometrics Commissioner; successful pilots with low false positive rates and full compliance reporting. No legal challenges.
involved_parties: UK Biometrics and Surveillance Camera Commissioner

example_name: Microsoft Tay Chatbot Shutdown (Analogous Unauthorized AI)
example_background: Though not biometric, Microsoft's 2016 Tay chatbot on Twitter was released into public interaction without sufficient safeguards, leading to rapid generation of inappropriate content. This illustrates risks of deploying AI in public spaces without pre-authorization and real-time oversight, analogous to unmonitored biometric scanning.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft shut down Tay within 16 hours, investigated internal processes, and enhanced AI safety protocols. No regulatory fines.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act high-risk system requirements

example_background: HireRight, a background screening company, processed facial recognition data from video interviews of job applicants in the Netherlands. The service analyzed video footage submitted by candidates to assess suitability for employment. This involved systematic processing of biometric data for identification purposes without explicit legal basis under national law allowing such use. The company operated the service for clients in the region from 2017 to 2021, affecting numerous individuals.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority imposed a €250,000 fine on HireRight in September 2024 for unlawful biometric data processing. The regulator determined the activity lacked a sufficient legal ground and violated restrictions on special category data.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI systems in employment)

example_name: Predictive Policing Tool Scrutiny in Netherlands
example_background: Dutch police explored the use of a machine learning algorithm to predict domestic burglaries by analyzing historical crime data and patterns. The tool aimed to allocate patrol resources more effectively. Development and testing phases involved collaboration between police units and tech providers. Prior to full deployment, concerns arose about potential biases and legal permissibility under national rules for law enforcement AI use.
process_and_outcome: The Dutch Data Protection Authority issued a warning in 2021, stating the tool constituted prohibited real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces unless explicitly authorized by national law. Police suspended further development pending legal clarification.

example_name: German National AI Strategy Compliance Framework
example_background: Germany established a national AI strategy in 2018, updated in 2020, emphasizing risk-based approaches to AI deployment in public sectors. The strategy includes provisions for high-risk applications in areas like healthcare and justice, requiring explicit legal bases and impact assessments. Federal and state authorities implemented guidelines mandating notification to oversight bodies for certain uses.
process_and_outcome: The framework has guided deployments, such as AI in social services, with successful notifications and approvals under state laws. No major fines reported; serves as model for EU-wide harmonization.
involved_parties: Federal Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action

example_background: TighterAI provided a facial recognition solution for access control in educational institutions in Finland. The system identified students and staff entering buildings. Deployment required assessment under national laws permitting such use for security objectives.
process_and_outcome: Finnish Data Protection Ombudsman authorized limited use in 2021 after review, stipulating strict data minimization and purpose limitation. Operations proceeded under oversight.

example_name: French CNIL Ban on Remote Biometric ID
example_background: Multiple companies offered real-time facial recognition services for public space surveillance in France, including crowd monitoring at events. These systems processed live video feeds to identify individuals against databases.
process_and_outcome: CNIL stated in 2020 that such uses are not permitted absent specific national legislation authorizing law enforcement exceptions. Services were ordered to cease, with warnings of sanctions.

example_background: ROBORDER developed robotics and AI for border security, including drone-based biometric identification capabilities. Tested at EU external borders with participation from multiple Member States.
process_and_outcome: Project completed in 2020 with strict pilot limitations and ethical reviews. National authorities in participating states provided explicit frameworks for testing, ensuring compliance through notifications and assessments.

example_name: Italian DPA Action Against Biometric Time Tracking
example_background: Companies in Italy deployed AI facial recognition for employee time and attendance tracking at workplaces, processing biometric data daily without designated national permissions for such employment-related uses.
process_and_outcome: Italian Garante issued injunctions and €20 million fine against one provider in 2022, requiring cessation and data deletion.

example_background: Amazon Web Services offered Rekognition, a facial recognition service, to law enforcement agencies including police departments in Oregon and Florida starting in 2017. The system analyzed images and videos for biometric matching against mugshot databases, supporting investigative activities in criminal matters. Concerns arose over accuracy rates, particularly for non-white faces, and the potential for misuse in surveillance without adequate oversight. Contracts allowed unlimited searches, with usage logs showing thousands of monthly runs by some departments.
process_and_outcome: In 2020, Amazon announced a one-year moratorium on police sales following public pressure and shareholder resolutions. Orlando Police Department discontinued use after a pilot revealed error rates. No direct fines imposed, but ongoing congressional inquiries influenced policy shifts.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 5(1)(g) biometric categorisation
EU AI Act Article 5(1)(d) real-time remote biometrics

example_background: Worldcoin launched a project in 2023 using iris-scanning orbs to collect biometric data from individuals worldwide, including in EU countries, in exchange for cryptocurrency tokens. Over 5 million scans were performed, creating a global iris database for identity verification. The process involved remote biometric analysis without explicit opt-in for all uses, and data was centralized for potential law enforcement access. Operations in Spain and Portugal drew immediate regulatory attention due to mass data harvesting in public spaces.
process_and_outcome: Bavarian Data Protection Authority initiated Europe-wide investigation in 2023. Spain's AEPD ordered a temporary halt to data collection, imposing a €3,000 fine initially. Worldcoin paused operations in multiple EU states pending compliance reviews.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 5(1)(h) emotion recognition

example_background: iBorderCtrl was an EU-funded project from 2016-2019 developing an automated border control system using AI-driven lie detection and facial recognition. The system analyzed micro-expressions, biometric signals, and categorized travelers into low/high-risk groups for police and judicial checks. Piloted at Hungarian and Greek borders, it processed passenger data in real-time for security screening, integrating with Schengen Information System data.
process_and_outcome: Project completed with recommendations for deployment, but faced criticism leading to scaled-back ambitions. No fines, but influenced stricter EU guidelines on biometrics. Follow-on systems adopted partial safeguards.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 5(1)(g) biometric categorisation

example_name: Palantir Technologies provided Gotham platform to police forces, including UK's Metropolitan Police and US agencies, for predictive policing. The system processed personal data, including biometrics from CCTV, for categorisation and risk scoring in criminal investigations. Deployed since 2010s, it integrated data from multiple sources for network analysis in Chapter 5 TFEU-equivalent activities.
process_and_outcome: UK Biometrics Commissioner review in 2020 found compliance gaps, leading to contract non-renewal in some areas. No fines, but transparency reports mandated. Palantir adjusted data handling protocols.
involved_parties: UK Biometrics and Surveillance Technology Commissioner

example_name: EU PNR Directive Implementation Challenges - Netherlands
example_background: The EU Passenger Name Record (PNR) Directive (2016/681) requires airlines to share passenger data with national authorities for analysis using AI pattern recognition and categorisation for serious crime and terrorism prevention. In the Netherlands, the system processed 17 million records annually, flagging individuals for police follow-up via automated biometric and behavioral profiling.
process_and_outcome: Dutch DPA ruled parts of implementation non-compliant with GDPR in 2021, ordering adjustments to profiling logic. No fine imposed, but system revised to enhance oversight.

example_background: Thales Group developed Cognytec, an AI platform for law enforcement biometric processing, including fingerprint and facial matching for judicial cooperation. Implemented with strict data minimization and audit trails for EU police forces, focusing on Chapter 4/5 TFEU activities.
process_and_outcome: Certified under ISO standards and GDPR compliance audits in 2022, with no incidents reported. Adopted by French National Police as best practice model.

example_background: IDEMIA supplied Automated Biometric Identification Systems (ABIS) to EU border and police agencies, processing multimodal biometrics for criminal identification without prohibited real-time remote uses. Systems emphasized post-capture analysis compliant with derogations.
process_and_outcome: Successfully deployed in Eurodac upgrades and national systems, passing 2023 EU interoperability audits with full adherence.

example_background: Meta Platforms, formerly Facebook, faced scrutiny over its practices of collecting and processing biometric data through facial recognition technologies embedded in its platforms like Facebook and Instagram. From 2011 to 2021, Meta's 'DeepFace' and subsequent systems automatically tagged photos and videos with facial recognition data, creating shadow profiles of users and non-users by scraping images from public posts and third-party sites. This processing involved billions of images to train AI models for identification, emotion detection, and targeted advertising, without explicit consent for biometric purposes, particularly affecting EU users under strict data protection rules.
process_and_outcome: The Irish Data Protection Commission (DPC) investigated Meta's biometric processing and in 2023 issued a €1.2 billion fine for GDPR violations related to data transfers, with separate probes into biometric uses. In the US, a class-action settlement in Illinois resulted in $650 million payout in 2021 for alleged BIPA violations involving facial scans. Meta suspended facial recognition in Europe in 2021 amid regulatory pressure but continued limited processing elsewhere.

example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, processed biometric data of millions of UK users, including children under 13, through facial recognition and behavioral tracking features in its short-video platform. The app's algorithm used device sensors, camera data, and video analysis to create personalized feeds, involving automated recognition of facial features and gait patterns without age-appropriate consent mechanisms. This affected over 1.4 million child accounts, with data shared with third parties for advertising purposes, raising concerns over unrestricted processing outside permitted exceptions.
process_and_outcome: The UK Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) investigated and in 2023 announced its intention to fine TikTok £12.7 million (reduced from an initial £15.9 million) for mishandling children's data, including biometric elements. The company was required to implement changes to default privacy settings and data processing practices.

example_background: Google's services, including Google Photos and YouTube, utilized facial recognition technologies to group images, suggest tags, and enable search by faces, processing biometric data of EU users automatically upon upload. The system created face models from user photos without explicit opt-in consent for biometric processing, affecting millions through cloud storage and AI-driven features, with data retained for model improvement.
process_and_outcome: The Italian Garante issued an order in 2020 prohibiting Google from processing biometric data via facial recognition in Italy, following complaints. Google was instructed to delete unlawfully processed data and faced potential fines up to €20 million. Similar actions occurred in other EU states.
involved_parties: Garante per la Protezione dei Dati Personali (Italian DPA)

example_background: Illumina, a genomics company, used facial recognition software from third-party vendors to monitor employee attendance and verify identities at its facilities, capturing and storing biometric data such as face scans without providing required notices or obtaining written consent releases from employees. This processing continued for years, involving thousands of workers whose data was retained in systems without a compliant destruction policy.
process_and_outcome: A class-action lawsuit in Illinois under BIPA resulted in a $10.5 million settlement in 2023, with Illumina agreeing to enhance privacy policies and delete stored biometric data. The case highlighted gaps in employee biometric protections.
