example_background: In March 2018, an Uber autonomous vehicle test unit operated without a safety driver actively monitoring during a nighttime test in Tempe, Arizona. The vehicle, equipped with sensing systems and software for navigation, struck and killed a pedestrian pushing a bicycle across a roadway obscured by darkness. The system's perception algorithms did not correctly identify the pedestrian as an obstacle, and operational data logs captured the sequence of events leading to the collision. Following the incident, Uber suspended all self-driving tests in Arizona and faced demands for detailed records on the vehicle's development, training data, testing protocols, and real-time logging capabilities to reconstruct the failure.
process_and_outcome: National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) conducted an investigation, releasing a report in 2019 that highlighted deficiencies in documentation and logging. Uber settled with the victim's family for an undisclosed amount, resumed limited testing with enhanced logging after implementing changes, and the case influenced state-level autonomous vehicle regulations.

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Crash Investigations - NHTSA Probes
example_background: Multiple fatal crashes involving Tesla vehicles using Autopilot have occurred since 2016, including a 2016 incident where a Model S collided with a tractor-trailer due to the system's failure to detect the crossing vehicle under bright sunlight. Tesla maintains event data recorders (EDRs) that log parameters like Autopilot engagement, driver inputs, and camera feeds for 10 seconds pre-crash. NHTSA investigations required Tesla to provide comprehensive technical documentation on neural network training datasets, validation processes, risk assessments, and continuous log updates to evaluate system performance across diverse conditions.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA opened defect investigations in 2021, leading to a 2023 recall of 2 million vehicles for software updates. Tesla submitted detailed logs and documentation, resulting in mandated improvements to driver monitoring and reporting requirements without direct fines.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: NHTSA Standing General Order on Crash Reporting

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition FTC Enforcement
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition system by scraping billions of images from public websites without consent, training models on this vast dataset to generate biometric embeddings. The company maintained internal records of data sourcing, model training pipelines, and performance metrics but faced scrutiny for lack of transparency in providing accessible technical documentation on biases, error rates, and ongoing monitoring logs to customers, primarily law enforcement agencies.
process_and_outcome: FTC settled in 2022 with a $20 million fine (stayed pending bankruptcy) and orders for comprehensive documentation, deletion of unauthorized data, and establishment of logging for all queries and matches to enable audits.

example_name: Apple Differential Privacy in iOS Health Features
example_background: Apple implemented differential privacy techniques in iOS features like emoji suggestions and health research data aggregation starting in 2016. The system collects anonymized usage logs from millions of devices, with technical documentation detailing noise injection methods, training processes on synthetic data, validation against privacy budgets, and continuous monitoring of epsilon parameters to ensure utility without compromising individual privacy throughout the system's deployment.
process_and_outcome: Features rolled out successfully, with annual privacy reports and peer-reviewed papers validating the approach. No incidents or fines, serving as an industry benchmark for high-risk AI documentation.

example_background: The COMPAS system, used by U.S. courts for recidivism risk assessment, employed machine learning models trained on criminal history and demographic data. ProPublica analysis in 2016 revealed racial disparities in predictions, raising questions about the opacity of training processes, validation datasets, algorithmic details, and absence of comprehensive logs for individual assessments to enable traceability and bias monitoring throughout operational use.
process_and_outcome: Northpointe (now Equivant) defended the system but faced lawsuits and policy changes in jurisdictions like Wisconsin. No fines, but led to mandates for algorithmic transparency in judicial AI.

example_name: Google Healthcare AI Technical Documentation under EU AI Act Prep
example_background: Google's DeepMind and Verily units developing AI for medical imaging and predictive diagnostics maintain detailed technical files covering data curation from clinical trials, model training with federated learning, extensive validation on diverse populations, cybersecurity-integrated logging of all predictions, and risk management systems. Documentation is structured for regulatory submission, with automatic event recording updated in real-time throughout deployment.
process_and_outcome: Approved for use in NHS trusts in the UK, with ongoing audits confirming documentation sufficiency. Positions Google ahead of EU AI Act high-risk requirements.
involved_parties: Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA)

example_name: Google GDPR Fine for Ad Personalization
example_background: Google was investigated for processing personal data of European users for personalized advertising without a valid legal basis. Between 2018 and 2020, Google's consent mechanism bundled multiple data processing purposes into a single 'Accept All' button, making it difficult for users to consent granularly. The company processed location data, browsing history, and device information extensively for ad targeting, without implementing data minimisation or clear privacy controls by design in its advertising algorithms.
process_and_outcome: The French CNIL fined Google €50 million in 2019, followed by a €100 million fine in 2022 for cookies violations. Additional investigations by other EU authorities led to further scrutiny and required changes to consent mechanisms.

example_background: Microsoft developed federated learning techniques for healthcare AI models, such as in its InnerEye project for radiotherapy planning. Instead of centralizing patient scan data, the training process sends model updates to edge devices at hospitals, where algorithms process data locally. Only aggregated, anonymised parameter updates are sent back to the central server, preventing raw personal health data transmission. This ensures compliance with strict data protection during AI training across distributed parties.
process_and_outcome: The technology has been deployed in partnerships with NHS trusts in the UK, receiving positive evaluations for privacy preservation. No violations reported; adopted as a best practice in medical AI.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act high-risk AI requirements

example_name: Amazon GDPR Fine for Employee Data Access
example_background: Amazon employees in Romania accessed and retained personal data of European customers without business justification between 2018 and 2021. Warehouse workers' courier data, delivery addresses, and payment details were stored unnecessarily, violating data minimisation. The systems lacked privacy by design to limit access and automate deletion.
process_and_outcome: Luxembourg's CNPD fined Amazon €746 million in 2021, one of the largest GDPR penalties. Amazon contested the fine and made system changes.

example_background: OpenMined's PySyft library enables federated learning and secure multi-party computation for AI training on sensitive data without data movement. Users train models on private datasets (e.g., medical records) by keeping data local and exchanging only encrypted gradients or proofs. This supports data minimisation and privacy by design, allowing collaborative AI development without raw data sharing.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by organizations like the Linux Foundation and used in projects with hospitals. Recognized in academic papers and EU-funded privacy initiatives as a compliance enabler.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (data governance, high-risk systems)

example_name: IBM Watson AI Health Bias Claims
example_background: IBM's Watson for Oncology AI system, developed for cancer treatment recommendations, processed patient data including health records categorized as special categories. Reports in 2018-2020 highlighted inaccuracies in recommendations, particularly for non-US patient populations, with reliance on training data skewed towards American demographics. The system ingested tumor profiles, genomic data, and patient demographics, leading to mismatched treatment suggestions. IBM invested over $4 billion but scaled back health AI ambitions by 2020.
process_and_outcome: Internal reviews and external critiques by STAT News led IBM to discontinue Watson Health sales in 2022. No formal fines, but the episode resulted in asset sales and reputational damage. Investigations by healthcare regulators focused on clinical decision support risks.

example_background: Facebook's advertising platform allowed targeting based on sensitive attributes like race, religion, and health via proxies such as ZIP codes and interests. A 2019 HUD lawsuit alleged the system enabled housing ads excluding protected groups, processing inferred special category data. Algorithms amplified biases from user data encompassing posts, likes, and profiles spanning 2.9 billion users.
process_and_outcome: HUD filed a landmark lawsuit in 2021. Facebook settled related class actions paying $14.25 million. Platform changes prohibited certain targeting options; ongoing DOJ investigation.
involved_parties: US Department of Housing and Urban Development

example_background: SAP developed an AI fairness toolkit for its SuccessFactors HR platform, enabling processing of applicant data including potential special categories for bias detection. The toolkit assesses models across protected attributes with statistical tests and provides correction mechanisms. Rolled out in 2021, used by enterprise clients for compliance with emerging AI regulations.
process_and_outcome: Adopted by clients without regulatory issues; praised in Gartner reports for proactive risk management. Supports ongoing model monitoring.

example_name: Uber AI Hiring Tool Bias Probe
example_background: Uber employed an AI system for initial candidate screening in 2018-2020, processing resumes and LinkedIn data. Internal audits found biases favoring candidates from certain universities and demographics, involving inferred age and gender data. The tool shortlisted 50% fewer women for software roles despite qualified pools.
process_and_outcome: Uber conducted internal remediation, diversified training data, and published findings. No external fines but contributed to broader DEI lawsuits settled for $10 million.

example_name: IBM Watson for Oncology Transparency Shortcomings
example_background: IBM Watson for Oncology, launched in 2016, provided AI recommendations for cancer treatments based on patient data analysis. Deployers in hospitals received treatment suggestions without sufficient insight into the model's evidence hierarchy, training data from 13 hospitals' biases, performance limitations on rare cancers, or scenarios where recommendations diverged from guidelines. Instructions lacked details on deployer actions affecting outputs and human oversight protocols.
process_and_outcome: Memorial Sloan Kettering distanced itself in 2021 due to inaccuracies. No major fines, but IBM pivoted to general AI research, and the case spurred oncology AI standards development.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: FDA Software as a Medical Device Guidance
EU AI Act High-Risk Medical Devices

example_name: Amazon Hiring Tool Bias and Transparency Failure
example_background: Amazon built an AI recruiting tool in 2014 trained on 10 years of resumes, predominantly male. The model downgraded women’s applications by penalizing resume terms associated with women. Recruiters lacked visibility into training data biases, scoring algorithms, limitation in diverse candidate pools, or instructions on overriding biased outputs, leading to flawed hiring decisions.
process_and_outcome: Amazon scrapped the tool in 2017 upon internal discovery. No external fines, but it influenced Amazon's AI ethics board formation and industry-wide bias guidelines.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act High-Risk Employment AI

example_background: EPIC Systems' sepsis prediction model in electronic health records used opaque algorithms to alert clinicians. Deployers received predictions without breakdowns of contributing factors like vital signs weights, model uncertainty in comorbidities, or performance drops in under-represented patient groups. Instructions omitted risks to patient safety from ignored alerts.
process_and_outcome: U.S. investigations post-2020 highlighted issues; EPIC enhanced explainability features in 2022 updates. Avoided fines through voluntary improvements amid FDA SaMD scrutiny.

example_name: Uber Self-Driving Car Fatal Crash - Elaine Herzberg
example_background: In March 2018, an Uber autonomous vehicle test unit struck and killed pedestrian Elaine Herzberg in Tempe, Arizona. The Volvo SUV equipped with Uber's self-driving technology was operating in autonomous mode with a safety driver, Rafaela Vasquez, seated in the driver's position. Video footage showed Herzberg crossing the road outside a crosswalk pushing her bicycle at night. The vehicle's sensors detected her approximately 6 seconds prior to impact but failed to classify her correctly as a pedestrian, leading to no braking action. The safety driver was looking down at her phone, distracted by a TV show streamed on her device. Post-incident analysis revealed that Uber's AI system had disabled emergency braking to reduce erratic behavior, a decision made by engineers. The vehicle was traveling at 39 mph in a 35 mph zone. This incident occurred during Uber's autonomous vehicle testing program aimed at developing commercial robotaxi services. Following the crash, Uber suspended all self-driving tests in Arizona and later globally for review.
process_and_outcome: Tempe Police investigation concluded the vehicle was at fault for not issuing an emergency brake command. The National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) issued a report criticizing Uber's safety culture, inadequate safety driver training, and system limitations. No criminal charges were filed against Uber, but safety driver Rafaela Vasquez was charged with distracted driving and endangerment. Uber settled with Herzberg's family for an undisclosed amount. The company restarted limited testing with human oversight improvements.

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Fatal Crash - Joshua Brown
example_background: In May 2016, Tesla Model S owner Joshua Brown died in a crash in Williston, Florida, while using the Autopilot feature. The vehicle failed to detect a crossing tractor-trailer against a bright sky, causing a high-speed collision under the trailer. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) investigation found that Autopilot's camera-based system could not distinguish the white trailer due to lighting conditions. Brown had driven over 45,000 miles using Autopilot, often in hands-free manner. Tesla's system lacked sufficient driver monitoring at the time, and logs showed no braking or steering input from the driver in the final seconds. This was the first known fatality involving Autopilot, highlighting issues in high-risk AI oversight during highway driving assistance.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA opened a defect investigation leading to a recall of 1.5 million Tesla vehicles for Cabin Camera-based driver monitoring upgrades. Tesla released software updates requiring more frequent driver attention checks via torque on the steering wheel and visual cues. No fines were imposed, but Tesla faced multiple lawsuits settled out of court. NHTSA closed the investigation noting improvements but ongoing concerns.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: NHTSA Standing General Order on Crash Reporting

example_name: Robert Williams Wrongful Arrest by Detroit Police Facial Recognition
example_background: In January 2020, Robert Williams was arrested in Detroit based on a faulty facial recognition match from a shoplifting surveillance video. The Detroit Police Department used facial recognition software that incorrectly identified Williams, a Black man, with 96% confidence. Officers did not independently verify the match through traditional investigation before arrest. Williams was detained for 30 hours, separated from his family, despite not matching the video description. This incident exposed flaws in deploying biometric identification without enhanced human oversight, particularly for minority demographics where error rates are higher.
process_and_outcome: Williams sued the city; the case settled. Detroit Police issued a memo restricting facial recognition use, requiring supervisory approval and corroboration before arrests. Michigan passed legislation banning police use of facial recognition.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Detroit Police Department Facial Recognition Policy

example_name: Nijeer Parks Wrongful Arrest by New Jersey Police
example_background: In April 2019, Nijeer Parks was arrested in Woodbridge, New Jersey, after facial recognition software falsely matched him to a theft suspect from surveillance footage. The system flagged Parks with high confidence despite physical differences including age, height, and weight. Police proceeded to arrest without separate verification by multiple officers, leading to Parks being held for about a month before exoneration. This case underscored risks in law enforcement use of biometric AI without mandatory dual-human confirmation.
process_and_outcome: Parks filed a lawsuit against Woodbridge and the vendor; settled for $300,000. New Jersey Attorney General issued guidance requiring human review and corroborating evidence for facial recognition identifications.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: New Jersey AG Facial Recognition Directive

example_name: EU AI Act Human Oversight Requirements Preparation - IBM Watson Health
example_background: IBM developed Watson for Oncology, an AI system assisting cancer treatment decisions, classified as high-risk under emerging EU AI standards. IBM implemented human-in-the-loop protocols where physicians must review and approve all AI recommendations before patient application. The system provides confidence scores, uncertainty flags, and evidence trails to guide oncologists. Training programs ensure clinicians understand system limitations. Post-deployment monitoring logs interventions for continuous improvement.
process_and_outcome: IBM's approach positioned it for EU AI Act compliance; no enforcement actions. System deployed in hospitals with oversight yielding improved decision accuracy per internal studies.

example_name: FAA Remote ID Drone Oversight Enforcement
example_background: The FAA mandated Remote ID for drones, requiring broadcast of identification and location data. In 2023, operators of high-risk drones (beyond visual line of sight) faced enforcement for lacking human oversight to monitor compliance. One case involved a commercial drone operator whose system failed to alert ground control to geofence violations, leading to airspace incursion.
process_and_outcome: FAA fined the operator $10,000 and suspended certificate pending implementation of real-time human monitoring dashboard. Operator retrained pilots on oversight protocols.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: 14 CFR Part 89 - Remote Identification of Unmanned Aircraft

example_name: Palantir Gotham Platform for Law Enforcement Oversight
example_background: Palantir's Gotham AI platform analyzes data for predictive policing and investigations. To address high-risk concerns, Palantir built mandatory human oversight layers: analysts must validate AI-generated leads with independent evidence, systems log all interventions, and two-person approval required for actions based on matches. Training ensures competence in interpreting outputs.
process_and_outcome: Deployed successfully with agencies like LAPD; internal audits show reduced false positives through oversight. No major incidents attributed to lack of oversight.

example_name: Amazon Rekognition False Positives in Police Trials
example_background: Orlando Police trialed Amazon Rekognition in 2017, resulting in 28 members of Congress falsely matched to mugshots at confidence scores above 80%. The system lacked mandatory dual-human verification, and trial parameters did not require independent checks, leading to overstated accuracy claims.
process_and_outcome: ACLU publicized results, prompting Orlando PD to abandon the technology. Amazon defended accuracy but added oversight recommendations in updated guidelines.

example_name: ENISA Report on AI Cybersecurity Practices
example_background: The European Union Agency for Cybersecurity published a 2023 report analyzing cybersecurity measures for AI systems, drawing from threat landscapes including data poisoning, model inversion, and evasion techniques. It examined deployments in sectors like healthcare and finance, highlighting integrated risk assessments that address both general digital product security and AI-specific threats such as adversarial perturbations.
process_and_outcome: Report provided voluntary guidelines adopted by several EU organizations. Demonstrated alignment between cybersecurity certifications and AI risk management, serving as reference for upcoming regulations. No enforcement actions; focused on proactive implementation.
involved_parties: European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA, publisher)
AI providers in healthcare and autonomous systems

example_background: In September 2022, a contractor's credentials were used to access Uber's internal tools, including AWS, GSuite, and Slack. The actor gained privileges leading to exfiltration of sensitive data like MFA seed files and internal repositories. The incident exposed weaknesses in access controls for cloud-based systems integral to operations.
process_and_outcome: Uber detected activity within hours, activated incident response. Attributed to teen hacker group Lapsus$. UK ICO fined Uber £146 million under GDPR for data protection failures related to the breach handling. US DOJ charged individuals involved.

example_name: CISA AI Data Security Roadmap Compliance
example_background: In 2024, CISA released an AI Data Security Roadmap outlining measures for federal agencies to secure AI data pipelines against poisoning and adversarial inputs. Several US agencies implemented these, including supply chain risk management for AI models sourced from third-party providers, aligning with broader product security requirements.
process_and_outcome: Adoption tracked through agency reporting. Served as model for harmonizing AI security with existing federal cybersecurity mandates. No violations noted; positioned as best practice.
involved_parties: US Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA)

example_name: Siemens MindSphere IoT Platform Security Certification
example_background: Siemens' MindSphere cloud-based IoT operating system underwent cybersecurity conformity assessment, incorporating AI analytics components. The platform addressed risks to digital elements including unauthorized alterations to operational behavior through integrated vulnerability management and AI-specific threat modeling like evasion attacks.
process_and_outcome: Achieved compliance with IEC 62443 standards and EU cybersecurity requirements. Declaration of conformity issued, enabling market access across EU. Used as case study for harmonized certifications.
involved_parties: German Federal Office for Information Security (BSI)

example_name: COMPAS Recidivism Algorithm Bias and Inaccuracy
example_background: Northpointe's COMPAS software, deployed since 2002 and widely used in US courts by 2016, employed AI algorithms to assess offender recidivism risk for judicial decisions on bail, sentencing, and parole. The black-box model scored individuals on a 1-10 risk scale based on survey data, criminal history, and demographics, purportedly with high predictive accuracy. A 2016 ProPublica investigation revealed inconsistencies: the algorithm was twice as likely to falsely label Black defendants as high-risk compared to white defendants, with error rates of 45% for Black recidivism predictions versus 23% for others. Performance varied across jurisdictions and over time without lifecycle monitoring or declared metrics in user instructions, impacting over 100,000 cases annually.
process_and_outcome: ProPublica report prompted lawsuits and policy reviews; Northpointe defended accuracy claims but restricted further audits. No direct fines, but led to moratoriums on AI in sentencing in some states. Eric Loomis case reached US Supreme Court, which declined review in 2017.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI accuracy and robustness)

example_background: Uber's self-driving car program in 2018 tested AI perception and control systems in Arizona, with vehicles equipped with lidar, radar, and cameras feeding into deep learning models for object detection, path planning, and decision-making. On March 18, 2018, a Volvo SUV struck and killed 49-year-old Elaine Herzberg pushing a bicycle at night. NHTSA investigation found the AI system failed to classify the pedestrian as human due to degraded performance in low-light conditions; the vehicle was traveling 39 mph, and the system braked only 0.3 seconds before impact. No redundant safety measures activated despite known limitations not fully disclosed in operational instructions. The fleet logged over 3 million autonomous miles prior, but robustness testing overlooked edge cases like backward-walking pedestrians.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA probe concluded in 2019 with no criminal charges against Uber, but company suspended AV testing. Civil settlement with victim's family undisclosed. Arizona revoked Uber's test permits. Program sold to Aurora in 2020.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI robustness and cybersecurity)

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Data Breach
example_background: Clearview AI built a facial recognition system scraping 30+ billion images from public web sources without consent, using AI models for matching against law enforcement watchlists. Launched in 2019, it claimed high accuracy rates above 99% in benchmarks. In 2021, a hacker accessed the full database via an insider account, downloading 500,000+ user records including API keys and credentials. The system's cybersecurity lacked multi-factor authentication and endpoint protections, exposing client data from 3,000+ agencies. Performance metrics were shared selectively, without lifecycle consistency assurances amid scraping bans from social platforms.
process_and_outcome: Multiple fines: UK ICO £7.5M (2022), Italy €20M (2022), France €20M (2022). Ongoing US class actions. Company continued operations with enhanced security claims.

example_name: IBM Watson Health Oncology AI Underperformance
example_background: IBM Watson for Oncology, launched 2016, used AI to recommend cancer treatments from patient data and medical literature, trained on 13 million+ pages. Piloted at Memorial Sloan Kettering and Indian hospitals, it promised state-of-the-art accuracy. Real-world deployment showed inconsistencies: recommendations mismatched expert consensus in 30-40% of cases, especially rare cancers, due to outdated training data and poor handling of multimodal inputs. No ongoing performance declarations or robustness updates provided to deployers; system failed FDA approval attempts amid lifecycle drift.
process_and_outcome: IBM sold Watson Health assets in 2022 for $1B loss. No formal fines, but reports led to program wind-down and hospital abandonments. Internal audits confirmed limitations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI accuracy and lifecycle performance)

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Crashes Due to AI Limitations
example_background: Tesla's Autopilot and Full Self-Driving (FSD) features, rolled out since 2014, rely on vision-based AI neural networks for lane-keeping, adaptive cruise, and obstacle avoidance. Marketed as advanced driver assistance with continuous over-the-air updates. From 2016-2023, NHTSA documented 736 crashes, 17 fatalities, often in low-visibility or cut-in scenarios where AI misclassified objects (e.g., sun glare, parked fire trucks). Performance degraded without explicit metric declarations in owner manuals; software versions showed varying robustness, with FSD beta exhibiting 1 intervention per 13 miles in tests.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA investigations ongoing since 2021; recall of 2M+ vehicles in 2023 for Autopilot fixes. DOJ probe into wire fraud claims (2022). Multiple civil suits settled.

example_name: Google DeepMind Acute Kidney Injury Prediction Deployment
example_background: DeepMind partnered with UK's Royal Free NHS Trust in 2017 to deploy Streams app with AI for early detection of acute kidney injury (AKI) from patient records. The Streams AI model analyzed electronic health records to alert clinicians, achieving 93% specificity in retrospective tests. Post-deployment 2017-2019, the system integrated seamlessly with lifecycle monitoring, declared performance thresholds (AUC 0.87), and provided clear deployer instructions on alert interpretation and false positive rates. Regular updates maintained consistency amid data shifts.
process_and_outcome: NHS pilot succeeded; expanded to 20+ hospitals. No incidents reported; model open-sourced elements for benchmarking. Praised in UK AI reviews.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (high-risk AI performance declaration)

example_background: In 2016, a former Uber engineer downloaded and took proprietary machine learning models related to self-driving car technology. These models were part of Uber's AI systems for autonomous vehicle navigation. The engineer then joined Uber's competitor, Waymo (Alphabet's self-driving unit), prompting Waymo to discover the transfer of over 14,000 files containing sensitive AI model data and trade secrets. This incident exposed vulnerabilities in access controls for AI assets within corporate environments.
process_and_outcome: Waymo filed a lawsuit against Uber in federal court. The case settled with Uber paying Waymo a 0.34% equity stake, valued at around $245 million at the time. Uber also agreed to implement stricter data security measures.

example_background: Clearview AI developed facial recognition technology by compiling a database of over 3 billion images scraped from public websites without explicit consent. The system's models were trained on this vast dataset, enabling law enforcement clients to perform reverse image searches. Multiple privacy regulators raised concerns over the unauthorized collection and use of biometric data in the AI training process, leading to operational restrictions in various jurisdictions.
process_and_outcome: Faced bans and fines from regulators including the Italian Garante (20 million euros), CNIL in France, and investigations by the FTC. Clearview paused operations in the EU and agreed to certain compliance adjustments.

example_background: In 2023, Elon Musk's X platform (formerly Twitter) updated its terms to allow use of public user posts for training AI models, including Grok by xAI. This led to lawsuits alleging unauthorized use of copyrighted content in datasets for large language models, exposing potential weaknesses in data sourcing practices for AI training infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Multiple class-action lawsuits were filed by content creators. The platform temporarily opted users out and faced ongoing litigation, with courts issuing preliminary rulings on data usage rights.

example_background: In 2024, hackers accessed DeepMind's internal systems using credential stuffing, obtaining documents on AI chip designs and research. The breach exploited weaknesses in authentication for ICT infrastructure supporting AI research operations, including access to sensitive model development environments.
process_and_outcome: Google (DeepMind's parent) confirmed the incident, contained it, and enhanced security protocols. No models were altered, but it led to internal audits and public disclosure.

example_background: Microsoft implemented multi-layered security in its Copilot AI tools, including data isolation, encryption, and continuous monitoring to protect against unauthorized access to training data and model inference endpoints. This approach integrates cybersecurity into the AI lifecycle, covering both AI-specific assets and supporting infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: The measures enabled Copilot to pass third-party security audits and comply with enterprise standards, avoiding incidents and supporting widespread adoption in regulated sectors.

example_background: IBM's WatsonX platform incorporates security controls from the design phase, such as adversarial robustness testing, secure model serving with encryption, and governance tools for data poisoning detection. This ensures resilience of AI systems against external influences on behavior or performance.
process_and_outcome: The platform received certifications and was deployed successfully in high-risk industries like finance and healthcare without major security incidents.

example_background: Anthropic developed Claude models using Constitutional AI, embedding safety constraints during training to maintain consistent behavior. Security measures include input filtering and model access controls to protect against exploitation of vulnerabilities in the AI system.
process_and_outcome: Independent evaluations confirmed improved safety metrics, positioning it as a benchmark for secure AI deployment.

example_background: Under the EU Cybersecurity Act, ENISA conducted a pilot certification scheme for 5G network components in 2022-2023, involving vendors testing conformity to security requirements for critical digital infrastructure products. Participating companies underwent third-party audits for secure design, vulnerability handling, and supply chain security, aligning with horizontal cybersecurity standards for products with digital elements in telecommunications.
process_and_outcome: The pilot successfully certified components meeting EUCC claims, providing assurance levels and feeding into permanent schemes. It demonstrated effective conformity assessment without reducing assurance for critical products, influencing the Cyber Resilience Act development.

example_background: In January 2022, the Russian hacking group Mandiant observed compromising AVEVA software (formerly Wonderware), used in industrial control systems, through a supply chain attack on a reseller. Attackers maintained access for months, targeting operational technology environments in energy and manufacturing, underscoring risks in software distribution chains for products with digital elements in critical infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Mandiant disclosed and remediated. Schneider conducted notifications and product reviews. It contributed to CISA's ICS advisories and heightened scrutiny on OT cybersecurity conformity.

example_background: The EU Cloud Services Scheme (EUCC), developed by ENISA under the Cybersecurity Act, certifies cloud providers at Basic, Substantial, and High levels based on conformity assessments covering security measures, transparency, and data protection for digital services. Providers like OVHcloud and IONOS achieved certifications, demonstrating adherence to horizontal cybersecurity requirements for cloud products with digital elements.
process_and_outcome: Audits by accredited bodies confirmed compliance, enabling use in public sector procurement. It established a baseline assurance model without compromising high-risk product standards, supporting market trust.

example_name: Uber ATG Fatal Autonomous Vehicle Crash
example_background: Uber's Advanced Technology Group (ATG) tested self-driving Volvo XC90 vehicles in Tempe, Arizona, equipped with LiDAR, radar, cameras, and AI software for perception, prediction, and control. On March 18, 2018, a test vehicle struck and killed 49-year-old Elaine Herzberg, who was crossing the road outside a crosswalk pushing a bicycle at night. The AI system detected her six seconds prior but classified her as an unknown object, failed to predict her path accurately, and did not initiate braking or evasive action. Safety driver Rachel Heinrich was monitoring but looking away at the time. The system's algorithms lacked robustness to low-light conditions, unusual pedestrian behaviors, and edge cases like bicycles, leading to no emergency response despite detecting an obstacle.
process_and_outcome: Tempe Police investigated, finding the safety driver inattentive. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) and NTSB probed; Uber suspended testing. NHTSA fined Uber $20,000 in 2020 for reporting violations. No criminal charges against Uber, but Heinrich pleaded guilty to negligent driving. Uber sold ATG to Aurora in 2020. The incident prompted stricter AV testing rules in Arizona and nationally.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Standing General Order on Crash Reporting

example_name: Tesla Autopilot Crashes with First Responders
example_background: Tesla's Autopilot and Full Self-Driving (FSD) features use cameras, radar, and neural networks for driver assistance, including highway navigation and traffic-aware cruise control. Multiple incidents occurred where vehicles failed to detect stationary emergency vehicles with active lights. On January 1, 2024, in Snohomish County, Washington, a Tesla Model S in FSD mode struck a stopped fire truck, injuring firefighters. NHTSA data showed six similar crashes since 2018, killing two people, due to the system's poor performance in low-contrast, high-glare conditions or with unusual light patterns. The AI lacked robustness to emergency lighting flicker and roadside scenarios, continuing forward instead of braking.
process_and_outcome: NHTSA opened a probe in January 2024 into 1 million+ Tesla vehicles after four crashes. Tesla issued software updates adding emergency vehicle detection. Ongoing investigation as of 2024; prior recalls included 2 million vehicles in 2023 for Autopilot issues. No fines yet, but enhanced reporting required.

example_background: Waymo, Alphabet's self-driving unit, operates robotaxi services in Phoenix, San Francisco, and Los Angeles using a multi-sensor suite (LiDAR, radar, cameras) with AI for perception, prediction, and planning. The system incorporates redundancy, such as dual compute systems, diverse sensor fusion, and fail-safe remote operators. It handles edge cases like construction zones, erratic pedestrians, and adverse weather through extensive simulation (billions of virtual miles) and real-world testing. Fail-operational design allows continued safe driving post-fault, with automatic pull-over for anomalies.
process_and_outcome: Waymo received the first U.S. national AV permit in 2020 and expanded commercial service without fatalities. NHTSA investigations post-minor incidents led to software refinements but no penalties. As of 2024, over 20 million autonomous miles driven safely.

example_background: Theranos, a health technology company, developed and placed on the market the Edison device, a system intended for performing comprehensive blood tests from small finger-prick samples. The company positioned itself as the provider responsible for deploying this high-risk diagnostic system into clinical laboratories and direct-to-consumer services. Operations expanded rapidly with claims of analyzing hundreds of conditions using minimal blood volumes, attracting significant investment and partnerships with major pharmacy chains like Walgreens for wellness centers. The system was used on patients despite lacking comprehensive independent validation, with internal concerns about accuracy raised by employees but overridden by leadership. Deployment proceeded without full disclosure of limitations, affecting thousands of test results.
process_and_outcome: The SEC charged Theranos with massive fraud in 2018, resulting in a $500,000 civil penalty and disgorgement of funds. The company faced criminal indictment; founder Elizabeth Holmes was convicted in 2022 on four counts of wire fraud and conspiracy, receiving a sentence of over 11 years in prison. Operations ceased, and assets were liquidated.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act

example_background: Illumina, a genomics company, proceeded with the acquisition of Grail, developer of Galleri, a multi-cancer early detection blood test classified as a high-risk AI-integrated diagnostic system due to its machine learning algorithms for signal detection and cancer origin prediction. As the acquiring entity, Illumina assumed responsibility for placing the system on the market post-acquisition despite ongoing regulatory scrutiny. The deal closed in 2021 for $8 billion amid concerns over market dominance, with Illumina designating itself as the accountable provider for Grail's deployment in Europe and beyond, handling quality management and post-market surveillance obligations.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission ordered divestiture in 2022, fining Illumina €432 million for gun-jumping violations. Illumina appealed but ultimately divested Grail in 2024. U.S. FTC blocked aspects of the merger, leading to prolonged legal proceedings.

example_background: Siemens Healthineers developed and took responsibility as the provider for placing AI-Rad Companion on the market, a suite of AI applications for radiology imaging analysis classified as high-risk under medical device frameworks. The system uses deep learning for tasks such as chest CT interpretation, brain MRI segmentation, and prostate MRI assessment. Siemens established a dedicated quality management system, conducted clinical performance studies across multiple sites, and implemented post-market surveillance protocols. As the designated provider, the company handles CE marking, risk management, and accountability for system updates and performance monitoring in hospital deployments worldwide.
process_and_outcome: Received CE Mark approval under MDR in 2020-2023 for various modules. FDA 510(k) clearances obtained. No major enforcement actions; continues commercial deployment with ongoing clinical validations published.

example_name: Zimmer Biomet ROSA Shoulder AI System
example_background: Zimmer Biomet assumed responsibility as the provider for the ROSA Shoulder system, an AI-assisted robotics platform for shoulder arthroplasty procedures. The system integrates AI for 3D preoperative planning, intra-operative guidance, and analytics using machine learning models trained on anatomical data. Zimmer Biomet conducted extensive validation studies, established a post-market clinical follow-up program, and designated itself accountable for regulatory compliance, software updates, and performance reporting. Deployed in surgical settings across Europe and the U.S., with provider-managed training for surgeons.
process_and_outcome: Obtained FDA 510(k) clearance in 2023 and CE Mark under MDR. Successful clinical adoption with published outcomes data; no regulatory penalties.

example_name: iCAD ProFound AI for Breast Cancer Screening
example_background: iCAD serves as the provider responsible for deploying ProFound AI, a deep learning software for digital breast tomosynthesis (DBT) analysis, identifying cancer risk and lesion locations. The system processes mammography images to provide case scores and heatmaps. iCAD manages the full lifecycle, including FDA submissions, clinical trials involving over 100,000 images, real-world evidence collection, and continuous performance monitoring through cloud-based updates. Deployed in radiology practices, with iCAD handling cybersecurity and interoperability compliance.
process_and_outcome: FDA 510(k) clearance in 2020; Breakthrough Device Designation. EU CE Mark obtained. Independent studies confirm performance; commercial success without enforcement.

example_background: Butterfly Network developed and acts as the provider for its handheld ultrasound devices integrated with AI for automated measurements, image optimization, and diagnostic support in cardiac, lung, and OB-GYN applications. The company established a comprehensive quality system, conducted multi-center clinical studies, and implemented lifecycle management for AI model updates. Butterfly handles regulatory filings, post-market surveillance, and provider obligations for systems used in remote and point-of-care settings globally.
process_and_outcome: FDA clearances for multiple AI features since 2020; CE Mark under MDR. Expanded adoption in telehealth; no violations reported.

example_name: FDA Warning to Aidoc AI Imaging Provider
example_background: Aidoc, provider of aiOS, an AI platform for medical imaging triage using algorithms to flag acute conditions like intracranial hemorrhage and pulmonary embolism. The company placed versions of the system on the market with claims exceeding cleared indications, modifying algorithms without new submissions, and distributing to facilities without adequate controls on intended use.
process_and_outcome: FDA issued Class I recall in 2021 for certain versions and Warning Letter in 2022 citing unauthorized changes and misleading promotion. Aidoc responded with corrective actions and submissions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act

example_name: GE Healthcare Edison AI Platform Accountability
example_background: GE Healthcare established the Edison developer program and takes responsibility as provider for deploying multiple AI applications on its platform, including Critical Care Suite for real-time patient monitoring with AI-driven alerts for deterioration. The company manages a centralized quality system, conducts joint clinical evaluations with partners, and oversees post-market changes, cybersecurity, and regulatory compliance for hospital-integrated systems.
process_and_outcome: FDA clearances for Edison apps since 2018; CE Mark under MDR. Platform supports over 50 AI apps; audited and compliant with no major actions.

example_name: Clearview AI Facial Recognition Database Deployment
example_background: Clearview AI developed a facial recognition system scraping billions of images from public web sources to create a massive database accessible via AI search for law enforcement and private entities. The company positioned itself as the provider deploying subscriptions without robust data governance, privacy controls, or lifecycle management, leading to widespread unauthorized use and data exposure risks.
process_and_outcome: Multiple fines totaling over $50 million: €30 million from CNIL (France) in 2022, £7.5 million from UK ICO in 2022, €20 million from Italy in 2022. Ongoing U.S. class actions and state AG investigations.

example_background: The British Broadcasting Corporation (BBC) operated its public-facing websites which were used by millions of users including those with visual impairments, hearing difficulties, and other disabilities. Multiple users with disabilities encountered barriers such as missing alternative text for images, incompatible keyboard navigation, low color contrast ratios, and lack of audio description for video content. These issues prevented equal access to news, programs, and on-demand services. Complaints arose from users relying on screen readers and magnifiers who could not fully navigate or comprehend content. The websites served public sector functions under broadcasting mandates, affecting a diverse audience across the UK. The problems persisted despite prior awareness and updates, impacting daily information access for affected individuals.
process_and_outcome: The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC) investigated following user complaints. In 2012, the High Court ruled that the BBC breached equality laws by failing to make websites accessible. The BBC was ordered to overhaul its websites, implement accessibility fixes, conduct audits, and report progress. Further monitoring ensured ongoing compliance, with remediation costs in the millions.
involved_parties: Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC, regulator)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities

example_background: Target Corporation's e-commerce website target.com offered shopping, product information, and services to customers nationwide. Individuals with visual impairments using screen readers faced obstacles including unlabelled links, inaccessible forms, missing alt text for images, and non-navigable menus. These barriers excluded blind and low-vision users from independent shopping and account management, equivalent to physical store access denials. The site received heavy traffic, amplifying the scope of unequal access. Similar issues appeared in search functions and checkout processes, persisting through multiple redesigns without full remediation.
process_and_outcome: A class action lawsuit was filed in 2006 by the National Federation of the Blind. In 2008, Target settled for $6 million in damages and attorney fees, plus committed $3.7 million to website modifications. Changes included WCAG compliance, testing with disabled users, and annual audits. The settlement set a precedent for online retail accessibility.
involved_parties: National Federation of the Blind (plaintiff organization)
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Title III

example_name: EU Commission EDPB Guidelines on AI Facial Recognition Accessibility
example_background: The European Data Protection Board (EDPB) reviewed AI-based facial recognition systems deployed in public spaces and services across EU member states. These systems often lacked accommodations for persons with facial differences, mobility impairments, or reliance on assistive devices, leading to higher false negative rates and exclusion from services like secure access or identity verification. Deployments in airports, banks, and government offices affected diverse users. The guidelines highlighted needs for alternative modalities like voice or PIN inputs integrated by design to ensure equal access.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, EDPB issued Opinion 3/2021 providing guidelines emphasizing accessibility in biometric AI under privacy and disability rights frameworks. Providers were directed to apply universal design, conduct impact assessments, and offer non-visual alternatives. Several operators adjusted systems in response to avoid enforcement.

example_background: Microsoft developed Seeing AI, a mobile app using AI to assist blind and low-vision users by describing surroundings, reading text, identifying objects, and recognizing faces via smartphone camera. The app incorporates universal design principles with voice output, high contrast modes, customizable speeds, and offline capabilities. Launched in 2017, it supports multiple languages and integrates with screen readers, serving millions globally. Features evolved based on user feedback from disability communities, ensuring inclusivity in AI-driven assistance.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft collaborated with blind users during development, achieving high user satisfaction scores. The app received awards like the 2018 Edison Award and widespread adoption without legal challenges. Ongoing updates maintain WCAG and Section 508 alignment.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act universal design principles

example_background: Robinhood Markets provided a trading app for stocks, crypto, and options to millions of users. Visually impaired individuals using screen readers like VoiceOver encountered issues such as unlabeled buttons, charts without descriptions, inaccessible gesture controls, and login barriers. These prevented independent trading and account monitoring, mirroring physical branch access denials. The app's popularity among younger users amplified exclusion risks for disabled traders.
process_and_outcome: In 2021, a class action under ADA was filed. Robinhood settled in 2022 for $7.5 million, agreed to app redesigns meeting WCAG 2.1 AA, independent audits, and a $1 million fund for affected users. Implementation timeline set to 18 months.

example_background: Google Research launched Project Euphonia to improve speech recognition AI for people with speech impairments from conditions like ALS or cerebral palsy. Traditional models performed poorly on atypical speech patterns. The project collected diverse datasets, trained personalized models, and integrated real-time transcription into apps like Gboard and Live Transcribe. Features include on-device processing for privacy and adaptability to individual voices, promoting equal access to voice interfaces.
process_and_outcome: Piloted in 2020 with users, expanded globally by 2023. Achieved 30% accuracy improvements in tests, with partnerships like ALS Therapy Development Institute. No regulatory actions needed due to proactive design.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act high-risk accessibility requirements

example_name: Air Canada Chatbot Disability Access Failure
example_background: Air Canada's customer service chatbot on its website handled bookings, refunds, and support queries. A user with cognitive disabilities input details about bereavement fares, but the AI provided inaccurate information on eligibility and process, leading to denied claims. The chatbot lacked simplifications, clear error handling, or human escalation paths suitable for users with intellectual impairments, resulting in financial loss and distress.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the Civil Resolution Tribunal (CRT) in British Columbia ruled Air Canada liable for $812 CAD in damages plus costs. The tribunal found the airline responsible for chatbot accuracy regardless of AI nature, ordering policy updates and training.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act (AODA)

example_background: Apple embedded VoiceOver, a gesture-based screen reader, into iOS and macOS since 2009, enabling blind users to navigate apps, websites, and system features via speech output and haptic feedback. It supports over 30 languages, rotor controls for quick actions, and developer tools for custom accessibility. Used in education, work, and daily tasks by millions, it exemplifies built-in AI-enhanced recognition for images and text.
process_and_outcome: Regular updates align with WCAG and user testing yield high accessibility ratings. Apple received multiple awards, including from the National Federation of the Blind, with no major lawsuits on core features.

example_background: Netflix streamed movies and series to subscribers worldwide. Blind users lacked access to visual content without audio descriptions, relying on external solutions or unable to enjoy programming independently. The platform's vast library included public-interest content, but omission of descriptions excluded users from cultural participation equivalent to sighted viewers.
process_and_outcome: A 2012 ADA lawsuit by the National Association of the Deaf and others led to a settlement. Netflix committed to adding descriptions for new original content and expanding library coverage, now including hundreds of titles with ongoing additions monitored by plaintiffs.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA) Title III
Twenty-First Century Communications and Video Accessibility Act (CVAA)

example_background: Samsung Electronics released the Galaxy Note 7 smartphone in 2016, featuring advanced battery technology. Shortly after launch, reports emerged of devices overheating and catching fire due to battery cell defects, including separator damage and manufacturing inconsistencies. The company's quality management system failed to detect these issues during pre-market testing and initial production validation. Post-market surveillance initially downplayed the scope, with only partial recalls issued before evidence mounted of widespread risks. Over 2.5 million units were affected globally, occurring in airlines, homes, and public spaces, prompting aviation bans.
process_and_outcome: Samsung issued a full global recall in September 2016, incurring costs over $5 billion. The CPSC and other regulators coordinated the recall. Internal investigations revealed quality control lapses, leading to revamped supplier audits and testing protocols. No direct fines, but significant reputational and financial impact.

example_background: Siemens Healthineers manufactured the Artis Icono and Zee product families of robotic X-ray systems for interventional procedures. Software versions failed to properly reconstruct 3D images, potentially leading to incorrect dose calculations and imaging artifacts during cardiac and vascular imaging. The quality management system overlooked validation of software updates, and post-market surveillance did not promptly capture field reports of malfunctions. Over 100 units worldwide were impacted, used in high-stakes medical environments.
process_and_outcome: FDA classified as Class 2 recall in 2022, requiring software upgrades. Siemens notified customers and provided field service corrections, resolving issues without reported patient harm.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU Medical Device Regulation (MDR) Annex IX

example_background: Roche Diagnostics supplied the V60 Plus ventilators during the COVID-19 pandemic. Defects in power supplies and software caused unexpected shutdowns and alarms during critical care. The existing quality management system, adapted from diagnostic devices, insufficiently addressed high-risk mechanical ventilator requirements, including post-market vigilance for field performance. Deployment occurred rapidly under emergency authorizations.
process_and_outcome: FDA Class I recall in 2022, highest risk due to potential deaths. Roche halted distribution, repaired units, and integrated enhanced QMS elements compliant with ventilator standards.

example_name: Medtronic Quality Management System Compliance Post-Recall
example_background: Following multiple device recalls, Medtronic implemented a comprehensive quality management system overhaul for its cardiovascular and diabetes products. The system integrated risk management, design controls, and automated post-market surveillance using AI-driven analytics to monitor adverse events globally. Documentation was standardized across EU MDR and FDA requirements, with regular audits ensuring conformity assessments. This addressed prior sectoral gaps in high-risk implantable devices.
process_and_outcome: FDA inspections post-2020 confirmed compliance, lifting warning letters. Medtronic reported zero major QMS observations in recent audits and received MDR certification for key products.

example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance Co. Ltd., a company based in China, faced requirements under EU data protection laws to designate a representative in the European Union for handling data processing activities. This was necessary to provide a local point of contact for supervisory authorities and data subjects regarding compliance with regulations applicable to services offered to EU users. The platform processes vast amounts of personal data from millions of European users through its social media application, including user-generated content, behavioral data, and location information. Prior to formalizing the representative, TikTok had been operating without a fully established EU-based entity for certain regulatory interactions, leading to scrutiny over accountability mechanisms. In 2023, TikTok updated its structures to appoint an EU representative headquartered in Ireland, aligning with obligations for non-EU providers to ensure regulatory access to information on data handling practices.
process_and_outcome: TikTok designated an EU representative in Dublin, Ireland, and notified the Irish Data Protection Commission. This action resolved ongoing concerns raised during investigations into child data protection and was part of broader compliance efforts that included a €345 million fine in 2023 for children's privacy issues, with the representative facilitating remedial measures and authority communications.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GDPR Article 27 (Representatives of controllers or processors not established in the Union)

example_name: Meta Platforms EU Representative under DSA
example_background: Meta Platforms, Inc., headquartered in the US, owns Facebook and Instagram, which provide digital services to hundreds of millions of EU users. Following the adoption of the Digital Services Act in 2022, non-EU providers were required to appoint an EU-based representative to handle regulatory obligations, including reporting on systemic risks, content moderation, and compliance data provision. Meta had previously relied on its Irish subsidiary for GDPR purposes but needed to formalize DSA-specific representation to ensure authorities could access information on algorithmic systems and platform operations.
process_and_outcome: Meta designated Meta Platforms Ireland Limited as its DSA legal representative in February 2023, notifying the European Commission and establishing processes for authority interactions. This complied with DSA entry-into-force requirements and supported ongoing transparency reporting.

example_background: X Corp., successor to Twitter Inc. based in the US, operates a social media platform used extensively in the EU for information sharing and public discourse. The Digital Services Act mandates that very large online platforms appoint an EU representative to serve as the primary contact for regulatory inquiries, complaints, and compliance verification, especially for systemic risk management involving AI-driven content recommendation systems. Post-acquisition by Elon Musk in 2022, initial delays in formalizing EU structures raised concerns among regulators about access to operational data.
process_and_outcome: In April 2023, X notified the European Commission of appointing X Corp EU Representative Services SL based in Ireland as its DSA representative. The Commission acknowledged this in July 2023 as part of X's designation as a VLOPS, enabling ongoing compliance dialogues.
involved_parties: X Corp EU Representative Services SL

example_name: Google GDPR Representative Fine by Belgium
example_background: Google LLC, established in the US, provides search, advertising, and cloud services involving AI systems to EU users. In 2019, the Belgian Data Protection Authority addressed Google's lack of a dedicated representative in Belgium for its 'Neighbors' feature, which processed street-level imagery data from EU residents without a local contact for supervisory access. This setup impeded authorities' ability to verify compliance with data protection impact assessments and consent mechanisms for automated processing.
process_and_outcome: The Belgian DPA fined Google €50,000 in April 2020 for failing to appoint a representative under GDPR Article 27. Google suspended the Neighbors feature in Belgium and appealed, but the decision underscored the need for EU representation.

example_background: Amazon Web Services (AWS), a US-headquartered cloud computing provider, offers AI and machine learning services to EU customers, including data storage and processing tools. To meet GDPR requirements for non-EU providers targeting the EU market, AWS established an EU representative to handle data protection queries, contractual obligations, and compliance documentation for services involving personal data processing on behalf of controllers.
process_and_outcome: AWS appointed Amazon Web Services EMEA SARL (Luxembourg) as its EU representative, integrated into its GDPR compliance program with Data Processing Addendums and transparency reports. This structure has supported successful Data Privacy Framework certification and avoided enforcement actions.

example_background: Microsoft Corporation, based in the US, delivers AI-powered products like Azure AI and Office 365 to EU organizations and consumers, processing extensive personal data through cloud infrastructure. Recognizing the need for a local contact under GDPR for non-EU establishments offering services in the Union, Microsoft structured its EU operations to provide authorities with direct access to compliance information, including AI system risk assessments and data protection measures.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft designated its Irish subsidiary, Microsoft Ireland Operations Limited, as the EU representative for GDPR purposes since 2018, facilitating multiple DPA investigations and achieving compliance certifications without representative-related fines.

example_background: Worldcoin, developed by Tools for Humanity (US/German entity with international operations), uses AI-powered iris scanning devices to create digital identities via cryptocurrency incentives, collecting biometric data from EU participants. The project's global rollout without a clear EU representative initially complicated regulatory oversight, as authorities sought information on high-risk AI compliance, data minimization, and consent validity.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, the Bavarian DPA in Germany initiated investigations, issuing a temporary order to halt data processing in Bavaria due to lack of adequate EU representation and compliance gaps. Multiple EU DPAs, including Spain's AEPD (€10k fine per violation potential), probed operations, prompting Worldcoin to appoint EU representatives and revise practices.
involved_parties: Bavarian State Office for Data Protection Supervision

example_name: Apple EU Representative for App Store
example_background: Apple Inc., a US company, operates the App Store distributing AI-enabled apps to EU users via iOS devices. Under the Digital Markets Act and DSA, as a gatekeeper, Apple was required to appoint an EU representative to manage regulatory interactions concerning algorithmic app ranking, sideloading compliance, and systemic risk reporting for its digital marketplace.
process_and_outcome: Apple designated Apple Distribution International Limited (Ireland) as its DMA/DSA representative in 2023, submitting compliance reports to the European Commission and implementing changes like alternative app distribution options ahead of 2024 deadlines.

example_background: Samsung Electronics launched the Galaxy Note 7 smartphone in 2016, featuring batteries supplied by two manufacturers, Samsung SDI and Amperex Technology Limited (ATL). Shortly after release, reports emerged of devices catching fire due to battery defects causing short circuits. The initial recall affected devices with SDI batteries, but fires continued in replacement units with ATL batteries. Samsung, as the provider of the high-risk AI-integrated consumer product, coordinated with importers and distributors globally to halt sales and manage returns. Distributors in markets like the US and EU suspended sales and issued safety notices based on Samsung's instructions. The incident involved the entire supply chain, with batteries produced in Asia, assembled into phones in Vietnam, and distributed worldwide through carriers and retailers.
process_and_outcome: Samsung initiated a global recall of 2.5 million units, costing over $5 billion. The US Consumer Product Safety Commission enforced a mandatory recall, and aviation authorities banned the devices on flights. No direct fines were imposed, but Samsung implemented enhanced quality controls and third-party audits for future products.

example_background: Apple Inc. continued selling iPhones in the European Union with proprietary Lightning connectors after the EU mandated a common USB-C charging port for smartphones by the end of 2024. The regulation aimed to reduce electronic waste and standardize chargers across devices. Apple, acting as manufacturer, imported products from China and distributed them through its stores and authorized resellers in EU member states. Importers and distributors placed these non-compliant devices on the market despite the approaching deadline, leading to availability of products not fully aligned with the harmonized standard. This situation arose from the transition period and Apple's delayed shift from Lightning to USB-C.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission opened infringement proceedings against Apple and other non-compliant manufacturers in 2024. Apple announced compliance by transitioning to USB-C, but faced potential fines up to 4% of global turnover if unresolved. Products were gradually phased out.

example_background: Boeing designed the 737 MAX aircraft with the Maneuvering Characteristics Augmentation System (MCAS), an AI-assisted flight control software, to compete with Airbus. The aircraft were manufactured in the US, exported to airlines worldwide, and distributed through sales to operators. Following two fatal crashes in 2018 and 2019 involving Lion Air and Ethiopian Airlines, investigations revealed flaws in MCAS software and inadequate pilot training disclosures. Foreign airlines acted as importers, and maintenance providers as secondary distributors in the aviation supply chain.
process_and_outcome: The FAA grounded the fleet in 2019. Boeing paid $2.5 billion in a US DOJ settlement, including a $243.6 million fine. The DOJ later alleged violation of the settlement, leading to a $455 million compliance agreement. Aircraft returned to service after software fixes and enhanced oversight.

example_name: ASML Export Compliance for Lithography Machines
example_background: ASML Holding, a Dutch company, manufactures extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines essential for advanced semiconductor production, incorporating sophisticated control systems. These machines are exported from the Netherlands to customers like TSMC in Taiwan and Samsung in South Korea. ASML acts as manufacturer and exporter, with customers importing the equipment. The company established robust compliance programs to verify end-use and prevent diversion, involving due diligence on importers and distributors amid US and EU export restrictions on technology transfers to certain countries.
process_and_outcome: ASML complied with tightened US export controls imposed in 2022-2023, halting shipments to China and implementing license verifications. No penalties were issued; the company maintained operations while adhering to restrictions, demonstrating supply chain management.

example_background: ZTE Corporation, a Chinese telecommunications firm, sourced US-origin components for its networking equipment and smartphones, which incorporated software-defined networking elements. The company used unauthorized distributors and resellers to procure these parts despite US export bans imposed for Iran-related transactions. Importers in China rerouted goods through third-country intermediaries, placing restricted technology into the global supply chain without proper verification.
process_and_outcome: In 2017, the US Commerce Department banned ZTE from purchasing US parts for seven years. ZTE pleaded guilty in 2017, paying $1.19 billion in penalties, the largest export violation fine at the time. Operations resumed after compliance overhaul.

example_background: Huawei Technologies supplied 5G network equipment with integrated AI management systems to telecom operators worldwide. Concerns over security led the UK and other countries to ban Huawei gear. Operators who had imported the equipment from China acted as distributors within their networks. Huawei established compliance teams to work with governments on audits, while operators removed and replaced equipment.
process_and_outcome: The UK government banned Huawei from 5G networks in 2020, requiring removal by 2027 at a cost of £5 billion to BT and Vodafone. No direct fines on Huawei, but sales halted, demonstrating adherence through cooperation.

example_background: Intel Corporation supplied microprocessors to original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) in Europe, who incorporated them into computers and distributed the products. The European Commission investigated loyalty rebates that foreclosed competitors. OEMs acted as importers and distributors, placing Intel-powered systems on the EU market under the New Legislative Framework for electronics.
process_and_outcome: In 2009, the Commission fined Intel €1.06 billion for abuse of dominance. The fine was partially annulled in 2022 by the EU General Court, reduced to €376 million, with appeals ongoing.

example_name: OpenAI and Microsoft Collaboration on GPT Models
example_background: OpenAI developed general-purpose AI foundation models such as GPT-3 and subsequent versions, which were integrated into Microsoft Azure cloud services and applications like Bing Chat and GitHub Copilot. These models served as foundational components for downstream systems handling tasks including content generation, code assistance, and search functionalities. Microsoft, as a deployer of services powered by these models, relied on OpenAI's technical documentation, safety reports, and API access to monitor usage and address potential issues in high-stakes applications. OpenAI shared model cards detailing capabilities, limitations, and risk assessments, while Microsoft conducted additional evaluations for its deployments. This arrangement involved ongoing exchanges of information on model updates, performance metrics, and incident reports to ensure alignment with emerging governance needs.
process_and_outcome: The companies established joint safety frameworks and public commitments to responsible AI development, including risk mitigation measures and transparency reporting, without facing regulatory penalties.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (Article 50 on GPAI obligations)
Voluntary AI Safety Commitments (US White House)

example_name: Stability AI Stability of Stability Diffusion Investigations
example_background: Stability AI released the open-weight Stable Diffusion models, general-purpose text-to-image generation systems downloaded millions of times and fine-tuned for applications in art, advertising, and biometric tools. Deployers adapted these models for uses including commercial image creation and facial recognition prototypes. Stability AI provided model cards with usage statistics, capability evaluations, and systemic risk disclosures, but faced questions over insufficient ongoing support for downstream users identifying unintended outputs in sensitive contexts. Multiple deployers reported challenges in tracing model behaviors back to the original weights.
process_and_outcome: UK and US regulators initiated inquiries into copyright and safety claims; Stability AI published updated transparency reports and engaged with authorities, settling some disputes without systemic AI fines.

example_name: Anthropic and Amazon Partnership for Claude Models
example_background: Anthropic created the Claude family of general-purpose large language models, offered via APIs and integrated into Amazon Bedrock, a platform enabling enterprises to build high-risk applications such as legal analysis tools and customer service agents. Anthropic supplied comprehensive model cards, constitutional AI training details, safety benchmarks, and mechanisms for deployers to report emergent behaviors. Amazon facilitated access while implementing logging and filtering layers, with both parties conducting joint red-teaming exercises and sharing vulnerability data to support compliance in regulated sectors like finance and healthcare.
process_and_outcome: Partnership resulted in industry-leading safety evals published publicly; no enforcement actions taken, praised in policy discussions.

example_background: Google launched Gemini 1.0, a multimodal general-purpose AI model powering Bard and integrated into enterprise tools for tasks like medical diagnostics support and hiring screening. Early deployments revealed factual inaccuracies and biased outputs in high-stakes uses, prompting scrutiny over the extent of Google's cooperation with downstream developers relying on the models. Documentation existed but was criticized for lacking detailed traceability for custom fine-tunes used in regulated environments.
process_and_outcome: Google paused features, issued apologies, and enhanced disclosures; faced congressional hearings but no EU fines as of 2024.

example_background: Meta released Llama 2 and Llama 3 as open-weight general-purpose language models, enabling widespread adaptation into chatbots, summarization tools, and components for high-risk systems in education and public administration. Meta provided model cards, safety reports, and acceptable use policies, but numerous deployers encountered difficulties in obtaining updated risk data for fine-tuned variants used in sensitive applications, leading to inconsistent handling of edge cases across the ecosystem.
process_and_outcome: Faced lawsuits over access terms and EU complaints; Meta expanded documentation but drew criticism for limited post-release support.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (cooperation along value chain)

example_name: Mistral AI and Enterprise Cloud Integrations
example_background: French startup Mistral AI developed Mistral 7B and larger general-purpose models, hosted on platforms like Azure and used in high-risk applications for code generation and financial forecasting. Mistral shared detailed technical reports, EU AI Act readiness assessments, and collaborated with cloud providers on logging standards to enable monitoring of deployed instances.
process_and_outcome: Positioned as EU AI Act frontrunner; participated in regulatory sandboxes with positive feedback.

example_background: Cohere built general-purpose language models for enterprise RAG systems used in legal research and HR screening, partnering with providers like Oracle Cloud. Cohere delivered customized safety toolkits, API monitoring endpoints, and joint compliance audits to support high-risk deployments.
process_and_outcome: Secured contracts with regulated firms; highlighted in industry reports for value chain alignment.

example_name: Inflection AI Pi Model Transfer to Microsoft
example_background: Inflection AI's Pi chatbot model was licensed to Microsoft for integration into Copilot, raising questions on continuity of support for users adapting it to high-risk personal assistant roles. Post-deal, documentation access persisted but coordination mechanisms were unclear.
process_and_outcome: FTC investigated acquisition for antitrust; approved with conditions, noting AI-specific concerns.

example_background: IBM acquired Watson Health and repurposed its general-purpose AI cognitive computing platform for healthcare diagnostics by modifying its intended purpose through partnerships with hospitals. Third-party deployers added their branding and customized datasets, effectively making substantial modifications that classified it as high-risk medical AI.
