Meta provided a permissive license that permitted users to access, use, modify, and distribute the model and its derivatives, with requirements for attribution to Meta and adherence to similar sharing terms for commercial use above certain usage thresholds. This release aimed to foster research and innovation by enabling developers and researchers worldwide to build upon the model for various applications, including chatbots and code generation tools.
process_and_outcome: The model was downloaded millions of times shortly after release, leading to widespread adoption in open-source projects. No regulatory enforcement occurred as the release aligned with emerging transparency practices, and Meta published system cards detailing capabilities and risks.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (transparency provisions for general-purpose AI)

example_background: EleutherAI, a grassroots research collective, released GPT-J in June 2021 as one of the first open-source alternatives to proprietary models like GPT-3. The 6 billion parameter model included full weights, architecture specifications, and usage instructions under the Apache 2.0 license. This allowed unrestricted access, modification, study, and redistribution without additional conditions beyond standard open-source attribution. The initiative sought to democratize access to advanced AI capabilities, enabling independent researchers to experiment and innovate without reliance on closed commercial APIs.
process_and_outcome: GPT-J gained significant traction with thousands of derivatives on platforms like Hugging Face. It contributed to numerous academic papers and tools, establishing a benchmark for open model transparency prior to EU AI Act formalization.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (open-source general-purpose AI transparency)

example_background: In February 2019, OpenAI developed GPT-2, a 1.5 billion parameter language model capable of generating coherent text. Instead of immediate full open release, OpenAI initially shared only smaller versions and API access, citing concerns over potential misuse in generating deceptive content. Full weights were released in November 2019 under a permissive license allowing study and use, but the phased approach withheld complete transparency initially. This decision sparked debates on balancing openness with safety in AI development.
process_and_outcome: After internal assessments and public dialogue, full model release proceeded without incident, influencing later discussions on responsible disclosure. No formal penalties, but it set a precedent for tiered openness.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (transparency for general-purpose models)

example_background: The BigScience workshop, involving over 1,000 researchers, released BLOOM in July 2022, a 176 billion parameter multilingual language model trained on 46 natural languages and 13 programming languages. All parameters, including weights, tokenizer details, and architecture, were published openly under the Responsible AI Licence (RAIL), which permits access, use, modification, and redistribution with attribution and ethical usage commitments. This collaborative effort emphasized transparency to support global research diversity.
process_and_outcome: Hosted on Hugging Face, BLOOM enabled cross-lingual studies and applications. The project documented its process in research papers, promoting reproducibility and community contributions.

example_background: Anthropic launched Claude family models starting in 2023, including Claude 3 with variants up to 200+ billion parameters estimated. Access was limited to API-only, with no public release of weights, architecture details, or training data. Users could interact via paid subscriptions, but modification and redistribution were prohibited, maintaining full control over deployment and preventing open study or forking.
process_and_outcome: Models achieved high performance benchmarks through API evaluations. No regulatory fines yet, but drew criticism for opacity amid calls for greater AI transparency.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (transparency requirements for GPAI)

example_background: Technology Innovation Institute (TII) in Abu Dhabi released Falcon 40B in May 2023, a 40 billion parameter model with full weights, architecture, and training details available under the TII Falcon License. This license allows use, study, modification, and distribution with attribution and non-competitive clauses for certain commercial uses. Trained on 1 trillion tokens, it was positioned as a high-performance open model to accelerate global AI research.
process_and_outcome: Downloaded extensively on Hugging Face, it powered various applications and research. TII followed with Falcon 180B, reinforcing commitment to openness.

example_background: xAI released Grok-1 in November 2023 initially as a closed beta accessible only to select X Premium subscribers. The 314 billion parameter Mixture-of-Experts model did not publish weights or architecture publicly at launch, limiting users to interactive use via API without modification or redistribution capabilities. Subsequent large model releases maintained this access model.
process_and_outcome: Beta usage informed iterations, but lack of openness limited external research. No EU enforcement as of yet, pending broader GPAI rules.

example_name: Sarah Silverman et al. v. OpenAI and Meta Copyright Suit
example_background: Comedian Sarah Silverman and authors Richard Kadrey and Christopher Golden sued OpenAI and Meta Platforms in 2023, asserting that their copyrighted books were ingested into training datasets for GPT models and LLaMA without authorization. Filed in California federal court, the complaints included evidence from internal leaks like the 'Books3' dataset, part of the Pile compilation containing over 196,000 books scraped from the internet. The authors noted that even open-weight models failed to disclose full training data compositions, raising issues about compliance with copyright law despite public availability of model parameters.
process_and_outcome: Judges dismissed parts of the cases in 2024 for lack of standing on DMCA claims but allowed copyright infringement allegations to advance. Partial settlements or ongoing appeals are reported, with emphasis on dataset documentation requirements.
involved_parties: Sarah Silverman, Richard Kadrey, Christopher Golden (plaintiffs)

example_background: In 2023, audiobook publishers including Penguin Random House, Simon & Schuster, and others sent cease-and-desist letters and pursued legal action against ElevenLabs, a developer of open-source text-to-audio AI models. The claims centered on the unauthorized use of protected audiobooks to train voice synthesis models, with outputs capable of mimicking specific narrators. Despite the models being released under open licenses with public weights, the publishers demanded transparency on training data summaries and policies addressing Article 4(3) copyright reservations.
process_and_outcome: ElevenLabs responded by updating its terms to prohibit infringing uses and implementing content filters, leading to some disputes being resolved out of court. No formal fines were imposed, but it resulted in revised model release practices.

example_background: Hugging Face launched the DataComp benchmark in 2023 alongside open-source model releases, providing detailed summaries of training dataset compositions used for vision-language models. This included breakdowns of licensed, public domain, and filtered content sources, along with policies for respecting copyright opt-outs. The initiative addressed gaps in open-source AI by voluntarily documenting data provenance despite no immediate regulatory mandate.
process_and_outcome: The project gained adoption in the community, with models trained on DataComp subsets achieving state-of-the-art results while demonstrating adherence to transparency standards, avoiding legal challenges.

example_name: Mistral AI EU Copyright Compliance Disclosure
example_background: French startup Mistral AI released open-weight models like Mistral 7B in 2023, accompanied by public statements on training data practices. They detailed using filtered public web crawls and licensed datasets, with policies to honor EU copyright reservations under Article 4(3) of Directive 2019/790. This transparency was maintained even as models approached systemic risk thresholds.
process_and_outcome: No enforcement actions; Mistral received EU regulatory nods and investments, positioning as a compliant open-source leader.

example_name: OpenAI Shift from Open-Source and Transparency Backlash
example_background: OpenAI initially positioned GPT-2 as semi-open but withheld full weights citing risks; later released open models like Whisper. However, halting broader open-sourcing for GPT-3+ due to safety concerns highlighted tensions. Leaked training details revealed heavy reliance on web-scraped data without comprehensive copyright compliance summaries, despite partial openness.
process_and_outcome: Internal policy shifts led to closed models; faced lawsuits and EU scrutiny, influencing AI Act exceptions for non-transparent systemic models.

example_background: The BigScience collaborative released the BLOOM multilingual LLM in 2022 as fully open-source, with 176 billion parameters public. They produced extensive documentation including a training data summary covering 1.6TB of filtered, multilingual web text, detailing deduplication, licensing checks, and copyright policy adherence. This served as a model for voluntary compliance in pre-AI Act era.
process_and_outcome: BLOOM gained widespread academic use without legal disputes, cited in EU AI Act discussions as best practice for open models.

example_name: EU Police Use of Clearview AI Database
example_background: Several European police forces, including in Germany, Austria, and Sweden, accessed Clearview AI's massive facial recognition database for criminal investigations despite the absence of explicit national laws authorizing such large-scale biometric processing for law enforcement. Queries were made for identifying suspects in cases ranging from theft to serious crimes, with data sourced from non-EU websites. This occurred between 2019 and 2022, bypassing standard data protection impact assessments and safeguards required for sensitive biometric identification.
process_and_outcome: The European Data Protection Supervisor (EDPS) and national DPAs launched probes in 2022-2023. In March 2023, the EDPS issued a formal notice to EU agencies to halt use, citing illegality. National investigations in Sweden and Austria led to temporary bans and data deletion orders, with ongoing proceedings for potential fines against police authorities.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Directive (EU) 2016/680 Article 10 (biometric processing for law enforcement)

example_name: UK Police Facial Recognition Trials Oversight
example_background: Between 2016 and 2023, UK police forces including London Metropolitan Police and South Wales Police conducted over 100 live facial recognition deployments at public events, scanning crowds to identify wanted individuals against watchlists. Systems from providers like NEC and Idemia processed live biometric data, with matches prompting manual review. Trials involved thousands of scans, with data retention policies varying by force.
process_and_outcome: Court of Appeal ruled in August 2020 that South Wales Police trials lacked sufficient legal framework, violating ECHR privacy rights; remedy was policy overhaul. Biometrics Commissioner reviews led to national guidelines in 2023 emphasizing necessity and safeguards. No fines, but mandated stricter compliance including DPIAs.
involved_parties: UK Biometrics and Surveillance Camera Commissioner

example_name: Dutch Police Facial Recognition Pilot Authorization
example_background: In 2020-2022, Dutch police ran a controlled pilot using facial recognition AI from Veriff and Paravision to analyze CCTV footage from 2019-2020 criminal cases, focusing on post-event identification of suspects. The system processed images against volunteer-provided photos and mugshots, limited to serious crimes, with data deleted after 6 months. Strict protocols included judicial oversight for database access.
process_and_outcome: Authorized by ministerial decree under the Dutch Police Data Act following legal advice. Pilot evaluation in 2022 showed 80% accuracy in tests; expanded under new law in 2023 with enhanced safeguards. DPA monitored compliance throughout.

example_background: Worldcoin, founded by Sam Altman, launched in 2023 a global project using 'Orb' devices to scan users' irises for unique biometric identifiers, creating a database for cryptocurrency distribution and identity verification. In Europe, devices operated in multiple cities, collecting iris data from thousands without fully disclosing law enforcement access risks or obtaining explicit Member State authorizations for sensitive processing.
process_and_outcome: Bavarian DPA investigated in 2023, issuing a temporary ban in Germany and €20 million fine proposal. Spanish AEPD imposed a 3-month EU-wide suspension in 2024 for special category data violations, ordering data deletion. Multiple national probes ongoing.

example_background: Since 2021, Finnish police have used facial recognition software from Idemia for post-event analysis of surveillance footage in criminal investigations, limited to serious offenses. The system matches images against a national database of suspects, with processing requiring prosecutor approval, data retention capped at 3 years, and annual audits for accuracy and bias.
process_and_outcome: Authorized under Finnish Police Act amendments aligned with EU law. 2023 DPA audit confirmed compliance with necessity tests and safeguards, reporting 250 successful identifications. Framework updated in 2024 to incorporate EU AI Act requirements preemptively.

example_name: OpenAI GPT Integration in Microsoft Azure AI
example_background: OpenAI's GPT models were integrated into Microsoft Azure as part of the Azure OpenAI Service, enabling developers to access large language models for applications in areas such as content generation, customer service chatbots, data analysis, code assistance, and creative tools. This integration occurred following Microsoft's multi-billion dollar investment in OpenAI starting in 2019, with public availability of the service launched in 2023. The service allows customization through fine-tuning and connection to enterprise data, supporting a broad spectrum of use cases across industries including healthcare for summarization, finance for report generation, and education for tutoring systems. Microsoft positioned it as a platform for building intelligent applications, with capabilities spanning natural language processing, vision, and speech, effectively transforming the system into one capable of serving varied functionalities beyond specific tasks.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission designated Azure OpenAI Service as a general-purpose AI model under the EU AI Act in 2024 due to its systemic risk profile and broad capabilities. Microsoft was notified and is required to comply with obligations for general-purpose AI models, including transparency reporting and model evaluations, with ongoing assessments by the AI Office.

example_name: Google Gemini Models in Vertex AI Platform
example_background: Google integrated its Gemini family of foundation models into the Vertex AI platform, a fully-managed machine learning service launched in 2021 and enhanced with multimodal capabilities in 2023-2024. Vertex AI supports deployment for tasks including text generation, image creation, video analysis, code completion, conversational agents, and enterprise search, allowing customers to build applications for marketing, e-commerce recommendations, legal document review, and scientific research. The platform's foundation model endpoints enable fine-tuning and retrieval-augmented generation across diverse sectors, with integrations into Google Cloud services like BigQuery and Looker, making it a versatile system for multiple purposes.
process_and_outcome: In 2024, the European Commission classified Google's Gemini models in Vertex AI as general-purpose AI models under the EU AI Act, subjecting them to specific transparency and risk assessment requirements. Google published technical documentation and model cards in response, aligning with preparatory obligations ahead of full enforcement.

example_name: Anthropic Claude Deployment via Amazon Bedrock
example_background: Anthropic's Claude models were made available through Amazon Bedrock, AWS's serverless platform for foundation models launched in 2023. Bedrock allows access to Claude alongside other models for building generative AI applications, supporting use cases such as intelligent chat interfaces, content summarization, code generation, financial forecasting, and agentic workflows in retail, media, and healthcare. Users can customize models with fine-tuning, Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG), and agent orchestration, enabling the platform to handle diverse tasks from creative writing to data extraction and multi-turn conversations, with enterprise-grade security features.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission designated Anthropic's Claude models as general-purpose AI models in 2024, notifying Anthropic of obligations including copyright data disclosure and technical documentation submission. Anthropic complied by releasing model reports and cooperating with the AI Office.

example_name: Meta Llama Models in Llama 2/3 Platforms
example_background: Meta released Llama 2 and Llama 3 as open-weight foundation models in 2023 and 2024, intended for integration into various systems. These models power applications through platforms like Hugging Face and AWS SageMaker, supporting tasks such as dialogue systems, instruction following, multilingual translation, coding assistance, and creative generation. Commercial partners integrate them into products for customer support, virtual assistants, and content moderation tools, with capabilities enabling broad adaptability across consumer and enterprise software.
process_and_outcome: Designated as a general-purpose AI model by the EU Commission in August 2024 due to its scale and versatility. Meta was required to provide detailed summaries of training data and model capabilities, fulfilling initial compliance steps.

example_name: Mistral AI Models in La Plateforme Service
example_background: Mistral AI launched 'la Plateforme' in 2024, integrating its Mistral Large, Mistral Nemo, and Pixtral models into a cloud service for European enterprises. The platform supports deployment for chatbots, data analysis, image generation, code synthesis, and customized agents, with features like fine-tuning and API access enabling uses in sectors such as public administration, finance, and media production, positioning it as a multi-purpose AI infrastructure compliant with EU data residency requirements.
process_and_outcome: Designated as a general-purpose AI model by the European Commission in 2024. Mistral AI provided required transparency documentation, including model evaluations, as part of early compliance efforts.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: GPAI Transparency and Systemic Risk Provisions

example_name: IBM Granite Models in Watsonx Platform
example_background: IBM integrated its Granite family of models into the watsonx platform, a generative AI and machine learning service introduced in 2023. Watsonx enables enterprise use cases including code generation, business analytics, customer engagement agents, and domain-specific tuning for finance, healthcare, and supply chain management. The platform's openness allows mixing models for varied tasks like text summarization, question answering, and multimodal processing, serving as a foundation for hybrid cloud AI systems.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission identified IBM's Granite in watsonx as a general-purpose AI model in 2024, prompting IBM to submit compliance documentation on model architecture and training processes.

example_name: Microsoft Phi Models Systemic Risk Designation
example_background: Microsoft's Phi-3 series of small language models were developed and integrated into Azure AI services starting in 2024, designed for efficient on-device and cloud deployment. These models support diverse applications such as mobile assistants, edge computing tasks, educational tools, and lightweight enterprise copilots, with capabilities in reasoning, vision-language tasks, and multilingual support, enabling broad integration into systems for productivity, accessibility, and IoT applications.
process_and_outcome: Designated by the EU Commission as posing systemic risks qualifying it as a general-purpose AI model in 2024, requiring advanced risk mitigation measures and reporting.

example_background: German AI firm Aleph Alpha integrated its Luminous models into the Lighthouse platform in 2023-2024, a sovereign AI cloud for European users. Lighthouse supports sovereign deployments for tasks including natural language understanding, generation, semantic search, and multimodal analysis, used in government, defense, and industrial applications, with emphasis on data privacy and customization for multiple sectors.
process_and_outcome: Listed among early GPAI designations by EU authorities in 2024, with Aleph Alpha engaging in compliance dialogues and providing model cards.

example_name: xAI Grok Model in X Platform
example_background: xAI released Grok-1 and subsequent models in 2023-2024, integrated into the X (formerly Twitter) platform for features like real-time information retrieval, image generation, conversation, and trend analysis. The model's design allows serving varied purposes including social media enhancement, research assistance, and entertainment, with API access enabling further integrations.
process_and_outcome: Under EU AI Act scrutiny as a potential GPAI due to integration scale; xAI monitoring for designation and preparing documentation amid global regulatory reviews.

example_background: EleutherAI, a collective of researchers, developed and released GPT-J, a large language model with 6 billion parameters, in June 2021. The model was trained on the publicly available Pile dataset and made fully available under the Apache 2.0 license, including model weights, training code, and evaluation tools. Distribution occurred through Hugging Face's open repository, allowing free download and modification without any associated fees or commercial services. No personal data was utilized beyond what was in the public dataset, and no technical support or monetized platforms were provided by the developers. This approach enabled widespread community use for research and experimentation.
process_and_outcome: The model gained significant adoption in the open-source community, with over 10,000 downloads in the first month and integrations into various projects. No regulatory actions were taken, and it served as a benchmark for accessible AI development.

example_background: In February 2023, Meta released LLaMA models (7B to 65B parameters) initially to selected researchers under a non-commercial research license. The models were leaked and widely shared on platforms like BitTorrent and Hugging Face. Meta later announced LLaMA 2 in July 2023 with a permissive license allowing commercial use for entities below certain user thresholds. However, Meta provided the models through a commercial platform requiring acceptance of terms that included data usage rights for improvement, and offered paid API access via partnerships.
process_and_outcome: The initial leak led to uncontrolled distribution, prompting Meta to formalize releases. LLaMA 2 was downloaded millions of times, but restrictions on scale and data rights led to debates on true openness. No formal enforcement occurred, but community forks emerged.

example_background: Mistral AI launched Mistral 7B in September 2023, a 7 billion parameter model outperforming LLaMA 2 13B on benchmarks. Released under the Apache 2.0 license, weights and code were hosted on Hugging Face for free access and modification. No fees were charged for the components, though Mistral offers a separate paid Le Chat service and enterprise support. Training data was not publicly detailed but emphasized efficiency without personal data monetization.
process_and_outcome: Achieved over 50 million downloads rapidly, topping Hugging Face charts. Company raised €385M in funding while maintaining open releases alongside commercial offerings.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act exceptions for free OSS

example_background: Hugging Face maintains the Transformers library, hosting thousands of open models under various OSS licenses. While models like BLOOM (176B parameters, BigScience release July 2022) are fully open, Hugging Face Inference Endpoints and AutoTrain provide paid hosting and fine-tuning services. Some model cards note data collection for platform improvement, potentially involving user data beyond interoperability.
process_and_outcome: Library has 100k+ stars on GitHub; paid services generate revenue. No specific enforcement, but raised questions on whether hosted services qualify as monetized components under open definitions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act data usage exclusions

example_background: BigScience, a 1,000+ researcher workshop hosted by Hugging Face, developed BLOOM, a 176B parameter multilingual LLM trained on the ROOTS dataset (1.6TB public text). Released July 2022 under Responsible AI License, weights were openly provided on Hugging Face without fees. The project emphasized transparency with full dataset and training details published, avoiding any commercial tying or paid services.
process_and_outcome: Downloaded extensively for research; served as model for collaborative open AI. No monetization or regulatory issues noted.

example_background: Anthropic, founded by ex-OpenAI researchers, developed Claude models focusing on safety. Unlike open releases, Claude weights are not public; access is via API with tiered pricing ($3-20/million tokens). Enterprise plans include custom support and data usage for improvement, with no open repository distribution of core components.
process_and_outcome: Raised $4B+ funding; Claude adopted by businesses. Model cards detail safety but no open access, aligning with closed commercial strategy.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act monetized AI exclusions

example_background: Technology Innovation Institute (TII) in Abu Dhabi released Falcon 40B in May 2023, trained on The Pile dataset. Provided under Apache 2.0 on Hugging Face with full weights, code, and details freely available. TII offered no paid services tied to the model itself, emphasizing public good and research utility without data monetization.
process_and_outcome: Quickly became top-downloaded model; leaderboard leader. Supported further open fine-tunes without commercial restrictions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act free component criteria

example_background: OpenAI, developer of ChatGPT and GPT series models, faced inquiries regarding the data sources used in training its large language models. In response to public and regulatory pressure, OpenAI published a high-level description of its training datasets, noting reliance on publicly available internet data, licensed datasets, and publicly available code repositories. The company stated that Common Crawl, which scrapes billions of web pages, forms a significant portion of the training data, supplemented by books from Project Gutenberg and other public domain sources. Specific details on copyrighted materials were not itemized due to competitive concerns, but OpenAI provided a narrative summary acknowledging web-scraped content potentially including licensed works. This disclosure occurred amid lawsuits alleging unauthorized use of copyrighted books and articles in training.
process_and_outcome: OpenAI voluntarily released the summary on its blog in 2023 without regulatory mandate. Multiple class-action lawsuits proceeded in US courts, with ongoing discovery into training data details. No fines issued specifically for transparency failures.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act (transparency requirements for general-purpose AI)

example_background: Meta released its LLaMA family of large language models as open-weight models in 2023. To accompany the release, Meta provided a model card detailing training data, including Common Crawl (filtered web data), C4 (Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus), GitHub repositories, and books from sources like Books3 dataset. The summary estimated 15 trillion tokens used, with proportions from web (60%), books (22%), and code (18%). Meta noted that data included potentially copyrighted material scraped from the internet but emphasized fair use defenses. This disclosure aimed to enable research reproducibility and addressed concerns from the research community.
process_and_outcome: Disclosure published alongside model release on Meta AI blog. No enforcement actions taken, though dataset Books3 faced separate lawsuits. Model widely adopted by researchers.

example_name: New York Times v. OpenAI Copyright Suit
example_background: The New York Times filed a lawsuit against OpenAI and Microsoft in December 2023, claiming that millions of Times articles were ingested into GPT models without permission or compensation. The complaint highlighted OpenAI's lack of detailed disclosure on which specific copyrighted works were used, making it difficult for rights holders to assess infringement. OpenAI's general statements about using 'publicly available data' were cited as insufficient for publishers to enforce rights. Evidence included ChatGPT reproducing Times articles verbatim when prompted.
process_and_outcome: Lawsuit ongoing in Southern District of New York as of 2024. Partial motions to dismiss denied. OpenAI released more details on data filtering post-suit.

example_name: Authors Guild v. OpenAI Class Action
example_background: Seventeen prominent authors, including John Grisham and George R.R. Martin, sued OpenAI in 2023 for training GPT models on pirated copies of their books scraped from shadow libraries like Library Genesis and Z-Library. OpenAI's disclosures listed 'books' as a category without specifying collections or handling of copyrights, complicating authors' ability to opt out or claim damages. The suit argued this opacity prevented enforcement of copyright rights.
process_and_outcome: Case consolidated with other publisher suits in New York federal court, ongoing in 2024. OpenAI defended on fair use grounds.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act data summary requirements

example_background: Google released its Gemini 1.0 model in 2023 with a technical report detailing training on web documents, books, code, and other public data. The report specified datasets like Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4), The Pile, and Massive Text. Google provided token counts (1.4-13 trillion) and proportions, with a narrative on synthetic data augmentation and filtering for quality. Copyrighted content in web data was acknowledged but not itemized to protect proprietary processes.
process_and_outcome: Published voluntarily on Google Research site. No associated enforcement actions.

example_background: EleutherAI, an open research group, created The Pile, a 800GB dataset used to train GPT-J and other models. They published a detailed dataset card listing 22 components, including BookCorpus2 (240k books), Project Gutenberg (books), arXiv papers, GitHub code, and filtered Common Crawl. Each subset included size, source, and potential issues like duplicates or PII. This granular summary enabled downstream users to assess and filter data.
process_and_outcome: Released openly in 2020; widely cited as best practice. No legal issues for disclosure itself.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act summary template principles

example_background: xAI launched Grok-1 in 2023, stating it was trained on web data and real-time X (Twitter) posts up to Q3 2023. The announcement provided a high-level narrative: 'vast amounts of text from the internet' expanded by X data for current events. No specific datasets or proportions were listed, citing the need to protect training methodologies amid competition.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary blog post; no regulatory scrutiny reported. Model released open-weights in 2024 with limited additional details.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act general-purpose AI rules

example_name: Authors Guild et al. v. OpenAI et al.
example_background: A coalition of prominent authors, including John Grisham, George R.R. Martin, and others, filed a class-action lawsuit against OpenAI, claiming the company trained its GPT models on pirated copies of their books obtained from shadow libraries like Books3 dataset. The suit described how entire novels were ingested into the training pipeline without licenses, enabling the AI to generate text in the authors' distinctive styles. Plaintiffs provided examples where ChatGPT produced continuations or summaries closely resembling their works. This action paralleled similar claims against Anthropic and was part of a broader wave of litigation addressing the use of unlicensed literary works in AI development, raising questions about the scale of data scraping from unauthorized sources.
process_and_outcome: Filed in New York federal court in 2023. Consolidated with other author suits. OpenAI moved to dismiss, citing fair use and First Amendment protections; court denied partial dismissal in 2024, allowing case to proceed.

example_name: Sarah Silverman et al. v. Meta and OpenAI
example_background: Comedian Sarah Silverman and authors Richard Kadrey and Christopher Golden sued Meta Platforms and OpenAI, alleging their books were scraped into training datasets like Books3 without permission. The complaint highlighted how Meta's LLaMA model and OpenAI's GPT series were built using copyrighted materials from internet archives, including full texts of published works. Examples included AI outputs replicating plaintiffs' humor and narrative styles. The case exposed the reliance on datasets compiled from torrent sites and web crawlers, which aggregated protected content indiscriminately.
process_and_outcome: Filed in California federal court in 2023. Judge dismissed direct copyright claims against OpenAI in 2024 for lack of standing but allowed claims against Meta to proceed; plaintiffs appealed.
involved_parties: Sarah Silverman, Richard Kadrey, Christopher Golden (plaintiffs)

example_name: Andersen v. Stability AI et al.
example_background: Visual artists Sarah Andersen, Kelly McKernan, and Karla Ortiz filed suit against Stability AI, Midjourney, and DeviantArt, claiming their artworks were used without consent in training Stable Diffusion and related tools. The complaint featured side-by-side images showing AI generating copies or derivatives of plaintiffs' distinctive styles from specific prompts, sourced from datasets scraping art-sharing sites.
process_and_outcome: Filed in California in 2023. Partial dismissal in 2024 for some claims, but core infringement allegations proceed to trial.
involved_parties: Sarah Andersen, Kelly McKernan, Karla Ortiz (plaintiffs)

example_background: Eight major newspapers, including Chicago Tribune, New York Daily News, and others owned by Alden Global Capital, sued OpenAI and Microsoft for training on their full archives of articles without licenses, alleging verbatim regurgitation and competitive harm to subscriptions.
process_and_outcome: Filed April 2024 in New York federal court. Early stages with defendants contesting jurisdiction.

example_name: Music Labels v. Suno and Udio
example_background: Universal Music Group, Sony, Warner sued AI music generators Suno and Udio for training on copyrighted sound recordings spanning decades, scraped from streaming services and YouTube, enabling mimicry of artists' voices and compositions.
process_and_outcome: Filed June 2024 in Massachusetts and New York courts. Ongoing with injunction requests.
involved_parties: RIAA, Universal Music Group, Sony, Warner (plaintiffs)

example_background: Anthropic secured licensing deals with News Corp and others to use content for training Claude models, compensating publishers and establishing formal permissions rather than relying solely on public data scraping.
process_and_outcome: Announced in 2024; multi-year agreements provide revenue to rightsholders and access to vetted datasets.

example_background: xAI, developing Grok, launched a portal allowing website owners to opt out of data usage for training, respecting robots.txt directives and providing mechanisms for rightsholders to reserve rights.
process_and_outcome: Implemented 2024; integrated into training pipeline to exclude opted-out content.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act TDM opt-out provisions

example_name: French BNF Opt-Out from AI Training
example_background: In September 2023, the Bibliothèque nationale de France (BnF) announced measures to reserve rights over its digitized collections following the EU DSM Directive. The national library implemented robots.txt blocks and updated terms of use to prohibit use of its 15 million+ public domain and modern works for AI training datasets. This action was taken to protect cultural heritage from unlicensed commercial scraping, providing a template for institutions managing copyright works.
process_and_outcome: BnF successfully enforced opt-outs, leading some AI providers to respect the reservations. No litigation ensued, but it influenced industry notices and scraping policies.

example_name: UK Publishers Association Robot Exclusion Guidance
example_background: In 2023, the UK Publishers Association issued guidance to members on reserving rights under the DSM Directive, recommending robots.txt implementation, updated terms of service, and notices prohibiting AI training use. Over 100 publishers adopted these, explicitly stating that content could not be used for machine learning datasets without permission. This collective effort aimed to ensure AI providers identify and respect reservations before training.
process_and_outcome: Many publishers reported reduced unauthorized scraping. The guidance informed ongoing dialogues with AI companies on licensing, preventing disputes.

example_background: Starting in 2024, OpenAI signed multi-million dollar licensing agreements with publishers such as Axel Springer (Politico, Bild), News Corp (Wall Street Journal), and Financial Times. These deals allow use of content for training ChatGPT while providing attribution and revenue sharing. OpenAI committed to respecting rights reservations and developing detection tools for licensed content, addressing prior scraping concerns.
process_and_outcome: Agreements implemented, with content integrated into models post-2023 cutoffs. This shifted from unilateral scraping to negotiated access, averting lawsuits.

example_background: xAI, founded by Elon Musk, publicly stated in 2023-2024 that its Grok models were trained on publicly available data, excluding paywalled or opted-out content. The company implemented policies to scan for and respect robots.txt directives and rights reservation notices per EU DSM standards. Grok's system prompts emphasize real-time web data over static training sets.
process_and_outcome: No major copyright suits filed against xAI to date. Approach praised for transparency, aligning with upcoming EU AI Act requirements.

example_name: Authors Guild v. Google Books (Analogous Precedent)
example_background: In a landmark case spanning 2005-2015, the Authors Guild sued Google for scanning and digitizing millions of books from libraries without permission for its Books project. Google argued fair use for search snippets. Though predating generative AI, the case established precedents on mass digitization for machine-readable training data, influencing modern AI disputes.
process_and_outcome: US Supreme Court ruled 4-2 in favor of Google in 2015, affirming fair use. However, it emphasized transformative purpose and no market harm, distinctions debated in AI cases.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: Influential for EU AI Act and Directive (EU) 2019/790

example_background: OpenAI developed and released the GPT-4 general-purpose AI model, which demonstrated capabilities in advanced reasoning, code generation, and multimodal processing across text and images. The model was trained on vast datasets and deployed through APIs and ChatGPT, reaching millions of users worldwide. Concerns emerged regarding potential uses in generating deceptive content, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, and societal impacts due to its scale and performance. As a large-scale provider, OpenAI faced obligations for transparency, risk assessment, and documentation under emerging AI regulations. The company provided technical reports detailing model capabilities, training compute, and safety measures, but initial evaluations highlighted gaps in comprehensive risk evaluations for high-impact scenarios.
process_and_outcome: The UK's AI Safety Institute evaluated GPT-4, identifying it as posing systemic risks requiring mitigation. The EU designated OpenAI as a systemic risk provider under the AI Act, mandating enhanced compliance including model evaluations and incident reporting. OpenAI committed to ongoing safety testing and released system cards with evaluation results.

example_background: Google released PaLM 2, a large general-purpose AI model powering Bard and other services, trained on extensive multilingual data. The model exhibited strong performance but faced criticism for insufficient public disclosure on training data provenance, compute usage, and potential biases. Internal documents later revealed limited pre-release testing for high-risk applications like misinformation generation, despite Google's size as a major provider warranting comprehensive obligations.
process_and_outcome: US lawmakers requested transparency reports; Google published partial details but faced Senate inquiries. No fines imposed yet, but contributed to calls for stricter GPAI rules in the EU AI Act.

example_background: French startup Mistral AI released Mistral 7B and Mixtral 8x7B, efficient open-weight general-purpose models outperforming larger proprietary ones. As an SME, they provided simplified compliance through model cards detailing architecture, training compute under 10^25 FLOPs, and curated data sources excluding personal information. Fine-tuning variants included modification logs, supporting value chain transparency without excessive costs.
process_and_outcome: Models gained EU approval for non-systemic status; Mistral joined voluntary codes of practice. No investigations; praised for proportionate compliance enabling rapid innovation.

example_background: EleutherAI, a non-profit collective, developed GPT-NeoX-20B as an open alternative to GPT-3, trained on The Pile dataset for research purposes. Participants explicitly avoided commercial applications, providing full technical reports, data provenance, and evaluation results. This approach encouraged voluntary adherence without imposing burdens on nonprofessional developers.
process_and_outcome: Model widely used in academia; no enforcement issues. Served as best practice for research exemptions in regulatory discussions.

example_background: Microsoft Research released Phi-2, a 2.7B parameter general-purpose model trained on 'textbook-quality' data, demonstrating high performance despite small size. As part of Microsoft but framed as research output, it included detailed cards on training data filtering, fine-tuning for specific tasks, and efficiency optimizations suitable for SMEs.
process_and_outcome: Hosted on Hugging Face with modification logs; adopted by startups. Aligned with simplified compliance paths, avoiding systemic designations.

example_background: Major music publishers including Universal Music Publishing Group, Concord Music Publications, and ABKCO Music sued Anthropic, the creator of the Claude AI model, for training on lyrics from songs without licenses. The suit claimed Anthropic's datasets incorporated copyrighted song lyrics scraped from the internet, with Claude capable of outputting substantial portions of lyrics upon prompting. This highlighted issues in using protected creative works in AI training without transparency or permission.
process_and_outcome: Lawsuit filed October 2023 in Middle District of Tennessee. Anthropic argued fair use and system design to avoid verbatim regurgitation. Case ongoing with discovery in 2024.
involved_parties: Universal Music Publishing Group, Concord Music, ABKCO (plaintiffs)

example_background: OpenAI published details on its data practices for models like GPT-4, stating that training data comes from licensed sources, public web crawls, and third-party partnerships. While not providing a detailed summary of all content, OpenAI committed to respecting robots.txt protocols and removing identified copyrighted material upon notice. This approach aims to balance innovation with copyright considerations amid ongoing scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: Voluntary disclosures in blog posts and system cards; no formal enforcement but used in defenses against lawsuits. Continues to evolve with watermarking and data filtering tools.
involved_parties: Content partners like Reddit, News Corp

example_background: Meta released Llama models with accompanying 'model cards' detailing training data sources, including public datasets and filtered web crawls. Meta emphasized deduplication and filtering for harmful or copyrighted content where identifiable, making high-level summaries publicly available to promote responsible AI development.
process_and_outcome: Public releases since 2023 include data summaries; faced lawsuits but disclosures aid compliance arguments. Ongoing updates to practices.

example_name: French Publisher AFP v. Clearview AI
example_background: AFP, a French news agency, sued Clearview AI for scraping and using over 20 million images, including AFP's copyrighted photos, to train facial recognition AI models. The practice involved mass web scraping without consent, feeding into a database sold to clients, analogous to generative AI training data ingestion.
process_and_outcome: Paris court ruled in 2024 that Clearview violated GDPR and copyright; ordered deletion of French images and €30,000 damages.

example_name: OpenAI GPT-4 Safety Evaluations and Red-Teaming
example_background: Prior to the March 2023 release of GPT-4, OpenAI performed extensive safety testing, including red-teaming exercises where external experts attempted to identify vulnerabilities. The model was evaluated for risks such as generating deceptive content or assisting in unauthorized activities. OpenAI documented these findings in a system card, detailing mitigations like reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and usage restrictions. This process was part of a broader effort to prepare the model for public deployment while addressing potential issues.
process_and_outcome: GPT-4 was released with safety reports published, demonstrating transparency. Ongoing monitoring and updates have been implemented based on post-deployment feedback, establishing a model for industry practices.

example_background: DeepMind developed AlphaGo, a system that achieved breakthroughs in game-playing AI. Throughout its lifecycle, the team implemented security measures for model weights, training data, and compute infrastructure to prevent unauthorized access. This included encrypted storage, access controls, and isolated environments during development and deployment phases. These steps ensured the integrity of the research amid high public interest.
process_and_outcome: The model was successfully used in matches without reported security incidents. DeepMind's practices contributed to its reputation for responsible AI development.

example_name: Stability AI Model Leak via GitHub
example_background: In late 2022, model weights and code for Stable Diffusion, a text-to-image generation model from Stability AI, were inadvertently made publicly available on GitHub. This exposure allowed widespread downloading and use of the model outside controlled channels. The incident occurred due to a misconfigured repository setting, bypassing intended access restrictions. Stability AI responded by addressing the repository and monitoring downstream uses.
process_and_outcome: The leak prompted community discussions on open-source AI risks. Stability AI issued updates to their distribution model, emphasizing controlled releases. No regulatory enforcement followed directly.

example_background: In March 2023, Meta's research model LLaMA was leaked online shortly after being shared under restrictive license terms with select academic users. Full model weights totaling 65GB were posted on torrent sites and forums, enabling public access. The leak stemmed from users exceeding license terms by redistributing files. Meta had intended limited access to mitigate risks associated with the model's capabilities.
process_and_outcome: Meta condemned the leak and pursued legal actions against redistributors. The incident spurred debates on licensing for powerful models, with subsequent releases like LLaMA 2 adopting more open terms with safeguards.

example_background: During the 2023 deployment of AI tools in Azure OpenAI Service, Microsoft established mechanisms to track and report incidents related to model misuse or failures. This included logging anomalous usage patterns and notifying authorities when thresholds for serious events were met. Protections involved endpoint security, rate limiting, and model guarding against prompt injections.
process_and_outcome: Internal incident logs informed product updates. Microsoft shared aggregated insights in transparency reports, aligning with emerging standards.

example_background: xAI built Grok with protections for its infrastructure, including securing model weights in controlled environments and implementing defenses against extraction attacks. Development involved physical and cyber access controls for data centers housing training hardware. These measures addressed risks from the model's scale and public-facing nature.
process_and_outcome: Grok was released without major security breaches reported. Practices supported scalable deployment.

example_background: Reports in 2023 indicated attempts to probe Google’s PaLM models for vulnerabilities, including efforts to extract training data via queries. Google maintained robust server protections, including anomaly detection and model isolation. Despite no confirmed breach, the incidents underscored needs for ongoing defenses in large-scale AI infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: Google enhanced monitoring and shared threat intelligence. No data loss occurred.

example_name: Google Gemini Ultra Compute Threshold Evaluation
example_background: Google's Gemini 1.0 Ultra model was trained using massive distributed computing resources estimated at around 10^25-10^26 FLOPs, incorporating pre-training on diverse datasets, synthetic data generation, and fine-tuning stages. Released as part of the Gemini family, it demonstrated high capabilities in reasoning, multimodality, and long-context understanding, powering applications with wide market reach including search enhancements and enterprise tools. The model's parameters exceeded hundreds of billions, and its deployment affected numerous end-users and businesses, prompting assessments of its generality and potential for significant internal market impact.
process_and_outcome: Google conducted internal evaluations and shared technical reports. Under anticipated EU AI Act implementation, such models trigger systemic risk presumptions, leading to requirements for model evaluations, incident reporting, and cybersecurity standards.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act GPAI Systemic Risk Provisions

example_background: xAI's Grok-1 model utilized over 10^25 FLOPs in training, encompassing pre-training on vast internet-scale data and fine-tuning for enhanced performance. Designed for broad generality, it supported text and reasoning tasks with access to real-time tools, reaching users through the X platform with significant market penetration. The training process included synthetic data methods, highlighting the cumulative compute as a proxy for high-impact capabilities.
process_and_outcome: xAI open-sourced model weights and shared training details, facilitating external capability assessments. This positions it for potential systemic risk designation under compute thresholds.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Systemic Risk Presumption

example_background: Meta's Llama 3, with variants trained on compute exceeding systemic risk thresholds, featured 405 billion parameters and capabilities in multilingual reasoning and coding. Released openly, it impacted developers and businesses globally, with training incorporating diverse modalities and scalability features. Evaluations focused on dataset size, user reach, and benchmark performance as predictors of generality.
process_and_outcome: Meta provided comprehensive documentation and risk evaluations. Compliance preparations involved benchmarking against state-of-the-art thresholds.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act High-Impact Capabilities Assessment

example_background: Microsoft's Phi-3 series, while smaller, incorporated advanced training techniques with cumulative compute approaching thresholds when scaled across iterations. Focused on efficiency, it demonstrated high capabilities relative to size, deployed in edge devices with broad market reach. Assessments considered algorithmic improvements adjusting compute-based presumptions.
process_and_outcome: Microsoft shared model cards and benchmarks, engaging experts on capability evaluations. No presumption triggered, but methodologies applied for ongoing monitoring.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Adjusted Thresholds for Efficiency

example_name: Mistral Large Systemic Risk Framework Application
example_background: Mistral AI's Large model utilized compute over 10^25 FLOPs, supporting multimodal inputs and enterprise deployments with high user numbers. Capabilities encompassed advanced reasoning and tool integration, evaluated via benchmarks reflecting generality and scalability.
process_and_outcome: Mistral released evaluations and committed to transparency reporting, aligning with presumption criteria.

example_name: AI Singapore Sea-Lion Model Capability Assessment
example_background: AI Singapore's Sea-Lion models scaled to high compute levels for regional languages, demonstrating generality in multilingual tasks with growing user base. Training incorporated efficiency gains, prompting threshold adjustments.
process_and_outcome: Capability benchmarks shared; compliance with evaluation methodologies demonstrated.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Capability Tools and Benchmarks

example_name: Google AI Principles and Partnership on AI
example_background: Google published AI Principles in 2018, committing to safe and beneficial AI development for general-purpose models like PaLM and Gemini. They collaborated through Partnership on AI, a multi-stakeholder group including industry, academia, and civil society, to develop best practices for risk assessment and mitigation. This involved creating taxonomies of AI harms, conducting impact assessments, and adapting principles based on feedback from diverse perspectives, including international approaches.
process_and_outcome: Principles integrated into product development, with annual reports on adherence and external reviews, avoiding major incidents through proactive measures.

example_name: Microsoft AI Safety Board and Standards
example_background: Microsoft formed an AI Safety Board and published standards for responsible AI, focusing on general-purpose models integrated into Azure and Copilot. The process included stakeholder consultations with regulators, academics, and NGOs to review codes covering fairness, reliability, privacy, and systemic risk mitigation. They adapted practices from OECD AI Principles and EU guidelines, establishing risk taxonomies for enterprise deployments.
process_and_outcome: Board oversight led to updated deployment policies, with transparency reports and collaborations enhancing model safeguards.

example_background: Meta implemented a Responsible Use of Technology policy and community review processes for Llama models, inviting open-source developers and researchers to participate in safety testing. They collaborated with external experts to document risk mitigations for open-weight models, including watermarking and usage guidelines, reflecting diverse perspectives on deployment risks.
process_and_outcome: Process resulted in model cards and safety reports for Llama releases, with community feedback driving updates.

example_background: Following release of Stable Diffusion, Stability AI faced concerns over generated content and established safety filters and collaboration with stakeholders to refine model training data and output controls. They engaged with researchers to assess diffusion model risks and adapted practices based on feedback, developing guidelines for responsible generation.
process_and_outcome: Implemented classifiers and updated models with safety features, releasing reports on mitigations.

example_name: OECD AI Principles Adoption by G7
example_background: The OECD published AI Principles in 2019, adopted by G7 countries, encouraging providers to develop codes covering risk assessment for general-purpose models. This involved collaboration with national authorities and stakeholders to establish a taxonomy of AI risks, with regular reviews to reflect state-of-the-art developments and diverse inputs.
process_and_outcome: Principles implemented in national strategies, facilitating voluntary codes among providers.

example_background: The European Commission conducted workshops with AI providers, national authorities, civil society, and experts to draft codes of practice under the AI Act. These sessions focused on general-purpose models and systemic risks, developing draft taxonomies and mitigation measures, incorporating international benchmarks.
process_and_outcome: Drafts published for consultation, forming basis for finalized codes with stakeholder input.

example_name: Baidu Ernie Bot Risk Assessment Shortcomings
example_background: China's Cyberspace Administration investigated Baidu's Ernie Bot for inadequate risk controls in its general-purpose model, which generated problematic responses despite deployed safeguards. The model lacked comprehensive pre-release assessments and ongoing monitoring, leading to public incidents.
process_and_outcome: Investigation resulted in suspension of services, mandated improvements, and public apologies.

example_background: Meta released LLaMA 2 in 2023 after performing safety benchmarks, including adversarial testing for jailbreaks, toxicity, and bias. The process involved over 100 external researchers in red-teaming and documented mitigations like refusal training and content filters. Lifecycle management included open-sourcing with safety instructions and ongoing community monitoring.
process_and_outcome: Public release of technical report and benchmarks; model adopted widely with integrated safeguards, serving as a compliance benchmark.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act model evaluation requirements

example_background: China's Baidu Ernie Bot, launched in 2023, was quickly jailbroken by users to generate restricted content on sensitive topics, revealing deficiencies in pre-deployment adversarial testing and cybersecurity protections. The model lacked robust governance for continuous risk assessment, leading to public incidents of guideline violations.
process_and_outcome: Baidu patched vulnerabilities post-incident and enhanced filters; faced regulatory review in China but no major fines reported.

example_background: xAI released Grok-1 in 2024 with documented safety testing, including adversarial robustness checks and alignment with truth-seeking objectives. Pre-market evaluations covered misuse potentials, with internal processes for monitoring and updates.
process_and_outcome: Transparent reporting on GitHub; model deployed with safeguards, exemplifying emerging best practices.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act systemic risk framework

example_background: Midjourney's Discord-based AI image generator, operational since 2022, enabled creation of violent and explicit imagery through user prompts, due to limited pre-launch adversarial testing and real-time moderation. Open access amplified systemic risks without lifecycle mitigations.
process_and_outcome: Community backlash led to policy updates and filters; ongoing investigations into harms but no formal enforcement.

example_name: OpenAI GPT-4o Systemic Risk Notification under EU AI Act
example_background: OpenAI developed and released GPT-4o, a general-purpose AI model with capabilities exceeding high-impact thresholds including compute operations during training surpassing 10^25 FLOPs. The model demonstrated advanced multimodal functionalities in text, image, and audio processing. Prior to full market release in May 2024, OpenAI engaged with EU authorities regarding its classification obligations under the AI Act. Training involved extensive planning with allocated compute resources on large-scale clusters, allowing early assessment of the FLOPs threshold. The company provided documentation on model architecture, training datasets, and risk evaluations to demonstrate specific characteristics potentially exempting it from full systemic risk classification. This process highlighted the importance of pre-release notifications especially for models with potential open-source considerations, enabling authorities to prepare for market placement.
process_and_outcome: OpenAI notified the EU AI Office within the stipulated two-week period after determining the model met high-impact thresholds. The notification included evidence on safety measures, red-teaming results, and mitigation strategies. The AI Office is reviewing the submission to confirm or rebut the systemic risk presumption. No fines imposed as proactive engagement occurred before enforcement began.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - Article 51 (Systemic Risk Classification)
EU AI Act - Notification Obligations for GPAI Models

example_background: xAI trained Grok-1, a large language model using over 314 billion parameters on massive compute clusters estimated at levels approaching systemic risk thresholds. Announced in November 2023, the model was positioned as a general-purpose AI with capabilities in reasoning and coding. Training planning included upfront allocation of GPU resources across data centers, providing advance knowledge of FLOPs exceeding 10^25. xAI considered open-sourcing the model post-training, which would complicate post-release compliance measures. The company evaluated internal risk assessments for high-impact capabilities like cybersecurity proficiency and persuasion before public disclosure.
process_and_outcome: xAI conducted pre-training notifications to relevant bodies and demonstrated through technical reports that model safeguards and limited deployment scope rebutted the systemic risk presumption. Model weights released openly without penalties. EU authorities acknowledged early engagement.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - GPAI Systemic Risk Thresholds

example_background: Anthropic's Claude 3 family, including the Opus variant released in March 2024, involved training compute estimated to meet or exceed EU AI Act systemic risk thresholds based on parameter count and FLOPs. The model exhibited high-impact capabilities in complex reasoning, tool use, and vision tasks. Development planning incorporated compute forecasting months in advance due to resource procurement. Anthropic emphasized constitutional AI safety frameworks during training. Consideration was given to open-weight releases, prompting early regulatory discussions to ensure compliance feasibility post-release.
process_and_outcome: Notification submitted to EU AI Office two weeks after threshold confirmation, with detailed evidence on model evaluations, safety benchmarks, and non-systemic risk arguments. Review ongoing; model deployed with API controls avoiding immediate penalties.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - Article 51(1) Presumption of Systemic Risk

example_name: Meta Llama 3.1 Systemic Risk Evaluation Delay
example_background: Meta released Llama 3.1 405B in July 2024, a model trained with compute exceeding 10^25 FLOPs on global data centers. Planning for training began over a year prior with H100 GPU allocations. The open-source release strategy raised concerns about post-deployment compliance enforcement. Internal assessments identified high-impact capabilities in math, coding, and multilingual tasks. Reports indicated Meta delayed formal EU notification until after partial open-weight publication, despite knowing thresholds were met during training phase.
process_and_outcome: EU AI Office initiated review after late notification; Meta submitted rebuttal evidence on model safeguards. Ongoing investigation into notification timing with potential fines up to 3% of global turnover if presumption upheld.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - Two-Week Notification Requirement
EU AI Act - Open-Source GPAI Obligations

example_background: Google's Gemini 1.5 Pro, launched February 2024, featured a 1 million token context window and training compute placing it at systemic risk levels. Resource planning for TPUs occurred well in advance. The model handled multimodal inputs with advanced reasoning. Google engaged regulators pre-release on classification, providing FLOPs calculations and risk profiles, particularly noting enterprise-focused deployment reducing public risks.
process_and_outcome: Timely notification to EU AI Office with comprehensive demonstration package. Classification review confirmed non-systemic status due to controls; no further actions required.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - High-Impact Capabilities Threshold

example_background: Mistral AI's Large 2 model, released July 2024, utilized compute resources meeting systemic thresholds with 123 billion parameters optimized for efficiency. Training involved European data centers with planned FLOPs tracked from inception. Open-weight licensing prompted specific focus on post-release obligations. Company provided early warnings during development phases.
process_and_outcome: Notification filed within two weeks of threshold breach confirmation. EU AI Office accepted risk rebuttal based on technical reports and safety evaluations; model proceeded to market.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - GPAI with Systemic Risks

example_background: Chinese firm DeepSeek released DeepSeek-V2 in May 2024, an open-source model with 236B parameters trained on undisclosed but high-scale compute likely exceeding EU thresholds if operating in EU markets. Planning details sparse, but resource scaling indicated prior knowledge. Open-source nature complicated hypothetical EU compliance post-release.
process_and_outcome: No pre-release EU notification observed despite potential market impact. EU authorities monitoring for enforcement; model distribution in Europe flagged for investigation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - Notification for Thresholds
EU AI Act - Open-Source GPAI Rules

example_background: Alibaba's Qwen2.5-Max, announced September 2024, featured training compute at exaFLOP scales with capabilities rivaling closed models. Global planning included EU cloud resources. Open-weight variants released without confirmed pre-notification, despite threshold awareness from cluster sizing.
process_and_outcome: EU AI Office requested post-release documentation; ongoing probe into notification compliance with potential classification enforcement.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act - Systemic Risk Presumption

example_name: OpenAI GPT-4 Systemic Risk Designation under EU AI Act
example_background: OpenAI developed and released GPT-4, a general-purpose AI model exhibiting capabilities that met thresholds for systemic risk as defined in the EU AI Act, including compute power exceeding 10^25 FLOPs during training and significant market influence on downstream applications. The model was deployed widely without prior notification to the European Commission about its classification status. The AI Office, through monitoring activities and input from the scientific panel, identified indicators such as the model's broad deployment across sectors including healthcare, finance, and public services, potential for indirect systemic impacts via proliferation to numerous users, and compute usage metrics reported in technical disclosures. This situation arose because initial assessments by OpenAI did not flag the model for mandatory notification, and public availability made it challenging to track all instances initially. The case highlighted gaps in proactive provider self-reporting for frontier models transitioning to systemic risk status.
process_and_outcome: The European Commission designated GPT-4 as a general-purpose AI model with systemic risk in August 2025, following evaluation by the AI Office and scientific panel. OpenAI was required to conduct fundamental rights impact assessments, perform model evaluations, adhere to transparency obligations including public disclosure of training data summaries, and implement cybersecurity measures. No fines were imposed initially, but ongoing compliance monitoring was mandated with potential for enforcement actions.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 51 (Systemic Risk Designation)
EU AI Act Title III Chapter 5 (GPAI with Systemic Risk)

example_background: xAI, founded by Elon Musk, trained the Grok-1.5 model using substantial computational resources approaching systemic risk thresholds under the EU AI Act. The model was integrated into the X platform (formerly Twitter) for real-time data processing and generation, reaching millions of users without prior notification to the Commission regarding its potential classification. Monitoring by the AI Office detected the model's deployment through public benchmarks and API usage statistics, revealing compute expenditures and model parameters indicative of systemic risk. The provider had not conducted or reported the necessary self-assessments, leading to undetected scaling of the model's influence across social media, content moderation, and information dissemination sectors.
process_and_outcome: In October 2025, the Commission exercised its designation powers based on qualified alerts from the scientific panel. xAI was ordered to notify future models promptly, submit detailed technical documentation, and establish a compliance framework. Remediation included retrospective risk assessments and a €10 million administrative fine for notification failure.

example_background: Google developed Gemini 1.0 Ultra, a multimodal general-purpose AI model trained with compute resources exceeding EU AI Act systemic risk thresholds. Recognizing the model's scale and intended broad deployment across Google Cloud, Workspace, and consumer products, the provider conducted internal evaluations and voluntarily notified the European Commission prior to full public release. This included detailed reporting on training compute, energy consumption, and foreseeable systemic impacts on sectors like education and employment. The process involved collaboration with the AI Office during pre-deployment reviews, ensuring alignment with transparency and risk mitigation obligations from the outset.
process_and_outcome: The Commission acknowledged the notification and confirmed the systemic risk classification without further designation proceedings. Google implemented required measures including adversarial testing reports and copyright data disclosure, receiving commendation for proactive engagement. No penalties were applied, and the model proceeded to market with certified compliance.

example_background: Anthropic trained Claude 3 Opus, a general-purpose AI model surpassing systemic risk criteria through high parameter counts and extensive fine-tuning datasets. The company established an internal systemic risk assessment protocol aligned with EU AI Act requirements, identifying the need for notification during development. Documentation submitted included compute logs, safety benchmarks, and impact analyses on critical infrastructure. This preemptive approach prevented undetected deployment and facilitated early feedback from the AI Office, demonstrating effective integration of regulatory monitoring into the model lifecycle.
process_and_outcome: Notification accepted in May 2025; model designated as systemic risk with Anthropic fulfilling all obligations such as model cards, evaluations, and incident reporting mechanisms. The provider was highlighted as a best practice example in AI Office guidance documents.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Title III Chapter 5

example_background: Meta released Llama 3 405B, an open-weight general-purpose AI model trained with compute exceeding 10^25 FLOPs, enabling widespread adoption by third parties without prior Commission notification. The model's open-source nature amplified its reach, powering applications in autonomous systems and decision-making tools across Europe. AI Office monitoring, supported by scientific panel alerts on benchmark performances and download metrics, uncovered the lack of classification reporting. Meta's focus on open access overshadowed regulatory self-assessment duties.
process_and_outcome: Designated as systemic risk in July 2025; Meta faced a €45 million fine for notification failure and was required to issue compliance updates, including weight release restrictions and user warnings. Ongoing audits mandated.

example_name: Mistral AI Mistral Large Designation Case
example_background: French startup Mistral AI developed Mistral Large 2, a general-purpose model with capabilities placing it at systemic risk thresholds due to training scale and enterprise integrations. Initial non-notification stemmed from ambiguous compute threshold interpretations. The AI Office's qualified alert system, triggered by scientific panel review of published specs, prompted Commission awareness and designation, underscoring the role of monitoring in identifying unreported high-risk models.
process_and_outcome: Designated in September 2025; Mistral complied by submitting technical documentation and implementing risk controls. No fine imposed due to cooperative remediation, but future notifications required.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Article 6 (Prohibited Practices Cross-Reference)

example_background: Microsoft's Phi-3 series, small but scalable general-purpose models, were assessed internally against EU AI Act criteria, identifying aggregate systemic risk from widespread Azure deployment. Microsoft notified the Commission proactively, providing evidence of compute usage and deployment safeguards, integrating AI Office feedback into release protocols.
process_and_outcome: Notification validated; models approved with systemic risk status and compliance certifications issued. Microsoft recognized for robust reporting processes.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Codes of Practice

example_background: Alibaba's Qwen 2.5 model, trained with massive compute, was made available via APIs and downloads without EU notification, despite systemic risk indicators from global benchmarks and Hugging Face metrics. AI Office monitoring via public data flagged the oversight, activating designation powers.
process_and_outcome: Designated in November 2025; €20 million fine and mandatory EU compliance officer appointment enforced.

example_background: Chinese firm DeepSeek developed DeepSeek-V2, meeting systemic thresholds. Following a qualified alert from the scientific panel, the provider self-reported and collaborated on assessments, demonstrating effective use of the alert system.
process_and_outcome: Designation confirmed with full compliance; no penalties, praised as model for international providers.

example_background: Google released the Gemini family of models, including Gemini 1.0 Ultra, Pro, and Flash variants, designed as multimodal general-purpose AI systems capable of processing text, images, audio, and video. These models were integrated into products like Bard and Google Cloud Vertex AI, serving enterprise and consumer applications. Google provided technical reports on model architecture, training compute exceeding 10^25 FLOPs, data curation processes, mitigation strategies for hallucinations and biases, and third-party audits for safety.
process_and_outcome: In December 2024, the European Commission designated Google Gemini as a systemic risk model under the EU AI Act, mandating enhanced transparency reports and adverse incident reporting. Google affirmed adherence to the regulation's requirements.

example_name: Anthropic Claude Codes of Practice Participation
example_background: Anthropic developed Claude models, including Claude 3 Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku, as general-purpose AI systems optimized for safety through constitutional AI techniques. The company engaged in early development of voluntary codes of practice proposed under the EU AI Act, publishing detailed model cards covering training methodologies, compute usage, evaluation benchmarks for truthfulness and helpfulness, watermarking for generated content, and mechanisms for user feedback integration.
process_and_outcome: Anthropic joined multi-stakeholder initiatives to finalize codes of practice ahead of the EU AI Act's August 2025 GPAI obligations deadline, positioning the documentation to demonstrate conformity. No enforcement actions were taken as of designation.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Recital 94 (Codes of Practice)

example_background: Microsoft released Phi-3, a series of small language models (3.8B to 14B parameters) trained efficiently on filtered synthetic and licensed data, intended for on-device and cloud deployment as general-purpose AI. Microsoft published comprehensive transparency reports including data sheets, risk assessments for privacy and bias, performance metrics on standard benchmarks, and deployment safeguards like content filters.
process_and_outcome: Classified as posing systemic risks by the European Commission in late 2024, Microsoft committed to providing quarterly transparency reports and cooperating with the AI Office on evaluations.
applicable_regulations_or_policies: EU AI Act Chapter V (GPAI Obligations)

example_background: Meta released Llama 2 and Llama 3 models as open-weight general-purpose AI systems, with parameter sizes up to 405B, trained on public internet data and licensed sources.
